observations.reason: Due to manpower constraints, the designation of additional officer-in-charge was not feasible; hence, the MGSO was unable to provide the required inventory, and the application for insurance coverage of the Municipality’s buildings was not facilitated as of CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to adopt strategies (e.g., information campaign, barangay caravan) to intensify the collection of real property tax and to persuade taxpayers to take advantage of early payment discount. Firm support of the local chief executive is necessary to encourage the local treasurers to pursue collection by all means; and We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant, Assessor, Acting Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records so that the amount of tax due per their respective records and the receivables presented in the Financial Statements are reconciled and properly valued.
observations.managementAction:
Management’s Comments:
The Municipal Accountant commented that they have already addressed audit recommendation no. 2 by setting up the RPT/SET receivables for CY 2025 at the beginning of the year, based on the list provided by the Treasurer’s Office.

The Acting Municipal Treasurer explained that they were unable to conduct tax collection caravans in the barangays due to the lack of available schedules for barangay assemblies. He also noted that the bulk of the receivables pertains to the delinquent taxpayers, which, if to be subjected to an auction as provided under Section 258 of R.A. No. 7160, would significantly reduce the receivables. However, the municipality has no authority to levy on real property, as this power is generally vested in the provincial government. Nevertheless, he asserted that the Municipality regularly meets or even exceeds the collection targets for RPT/SET set by the province.
observations.reason:
The Acting Municipal Treasurer explained that they were unable to conduct tax collection caravans in the barangays due to the lack of available schedules for barangay assemblies. He also noted that the bulk of the receivables pertains to the delinquent taxpayers, which, if to be subjected to an auction as provided under Section 258 of R.A. No. 7160, would significantly reduce the receivables. However, the municipality has no authority to levy on real property, as this power is generally vested in the provincial government.

Auditor’s Rejoinder:
The Municipality should coordinate with the Provincial Government to initiate proper legal procedures on the real properties with delinquencies as provided under R.A. No. 7160.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Mayor will require the Inventory Committee to: (a) attend training or seminar on property and supply management or coordinate and ask assistance from the Inventory Committees of other LGUs who have already successfully conducted physical count; and (b) give time and importance to the conduct of physical count of Inventories and PPEs despite of their busy schedule or workload so that the corresponding RPCI and RPCPPE could be prepared and submitted to the Audit Team within the timeframe. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Acting Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the property and accounting records, respectively, and ensure that these separate but related records are reconciled.
observations.managementAction:
Not Implemented (a) Not Implemented: No training or seminar on property and supply management was attended by the Inventory Committee in CY 2024. Also, they did not seek assistance from the Inventory Committee of other LGUs.

(b) Not Implemented: The Inventory Committee still failed to conduct the physical count of Inventories and PPEs thus, no RPCI and RPCPPE for CY 2024 were prepared and submitted due to their busy schedules and workload.

All were reiterated in Observation No. 3 in Part II of this report
observations.reason: No training or seminar on property and supply management was attended by the Inventory Committee in CY 2024. Also, they did not seek assistance from the Inventory Committee of other LGUs. The Inventory Committee still failed to conduct the physical count of Inventories and PPEs thus, no RPCI and RPCPPE for CY 2024 were prepared and submitted due to their busy schedules and workload. The property and accounting records were still not prepared and maintained due to lack of knowledge on how to prepare the same and manpower constraints.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Accountant to work on the unidentified item in the accounts with no details to provide any supporting documents to establish the balance and consider the applicability of the guidelines set forth under COA Circular No. 2016-005 so that appropriate action can be done for its final disposition.
observations.reason: The journal entry voucher on adjusting entry has yet to be submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: Establish clear cut guidelines on the selection and grant of AIFCS which may be aligned with the DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 02, series of 2014.
observations.reason: Copy of the guidelines has yet to be submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: The accounting unit to file the request for authority to write-off for dormant receivable accounts pursuant to the guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant submitted request for write off to the Audit Team on April 8, 2025 but this lacked supporting documents required in COA Circular No. 2023-008. The request was only attached with a general ledger and schedule but not certified by the accountant.

observations.recommendation:
Make a follow-up with PAO, and PDIC to fast-track the resolution of the case and the filing of appropriate charges against the persons found to be liable.

Communicate with Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas to determine the status and seek assistance for the recovery of the cash deposit.
observations.reason:
Copy of the follow-up letter has yet to be submitted to the Audit Team as proof that the audit recommendation has been complied.

Not Implemented

Copy of the communication letter has yet to be submitted to the Audit Team as proof that the audit recommendation has been complied.

observations.recommendation: Commence the process of disposal of unserviceable properties in accordance with the guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 89-296 and make sure that the properties presented under PPE are only those assets used in the operation of the LGU.
observations.reason: The disposal of unserviceable property has yet to commence as the RPCPPE as of December 31, 2024 listed unserviceable vehicles.

observations.recommendation: Expedite the implementation of 29 projects out of financial assistance received pursuant to the respective Memorandum of Agreement, Trust Agreement or Memorandum of Understanding which governs the utilization of funds and conduct regular monitoring and analysis to ensure that fund transfers are liquidated within the prescribed period depending on their nature and purpose.
observations.reason: The utilization report has yet to be submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE instruct:
The GSO to determine and reclassify from PPE to semi-expendable the properties that are below the capitalization threshold which are already issued to the end-user and those which are still in his custody for the proper classification of these properties and prepare a corrected RPCPPE and Report on the Physical Count of Semi-expendable Property (RPCSP); and
The Municipal Accountant to apply the provision of COA Circular No. 2024-006, based on the report of the GSO, to properly classify the properties below the capitalization threshold in order not to overstate the PPE accounts and not to understate the semi-expendable and/or expense accounts appearing in the FS.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere with the COA Circulars which prescribe the Chart of Account in recording the transactions of the Municipality in order to fairly present the FS.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list showing the names of the taxpayers or the RPTAR/ Taxpayers’ Index Cards. Also, we recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Assessor to provide the Municipal Treasurer the assessment rolls which will serve as her basis in the preparation of RPTAR/ Taxpayers' Index Cards.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the accounts and prepare the necessary adjustments so that the RPT Receivable and the SET Receivable are of the same amount which are also equal to the corollary accounts Deferred RPT Income and Deferred SET Income to fairly present the financial statements.

We recommended that the LCE:
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer and the Implementing Offices or focal persons to send notices/ demand letters to the concerned NGOs/POs and individuals for the immediate submission of liquidation report and/or collection and adopt other measures to recover the amount of fund transfers and receivables, such as seeking legal remedies, prior to its derecognition, hence the LGU could have additional funds to finance more developmental projects.
Require the Municipal Accountant to avail of the advantages of COA Circular No. 2023-008 and follow the procedures for the proper disposition and derecognition of dormant accounts as mandated by Sections 7 and 8 of the Circular in order to fairly present the FS.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Assessor to facilitate the immediate submission of the lacking supporting documents in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 in order to substantiate the legality, reasonableness and propriety of the amount paid of the acquired lot, hence to avoid possible incurrence of additional expenses.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Engineer to see to it that list of all on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year furnish the Audit Team at the beginning of the year and  provide a notice to the Audit Team within ten days, after the award of infrastructure projects or before its start that the project signboard was already posted in compliance with COA Circular No. 2013-004, thus, enable the Auditor to conduct timely verification and monitoring of the implemented infrastructure projects, thus deficiencies, if any, would  be communicated to Management for its immediate and appropriate action. More so, the constituents are also informed of such public concern, hence transparency for good governance is ensured.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to see it that the provision of EO No. 77 in the payment of DTE are strictly adhered to avoid over payment resulting to wastage of government funds. Likewise, we recommend the LCE to require the immediate refund of the overpaid DTE from the concerned individuals.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Treasurer to send demand letters for the immediate liquidation of the cash advances and ensure that the same is received by the concerned persons and seek legal remedies so that the unliquidated cash advances of those accountable persons who are no longer in service will be settled the soonest possible time.
Likewise, we recommended that the LCE remind the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to observe compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Section 89 of PD No. 1445 for the liquidation of cash advances within the prescribed period and that no additional cash advance shall be granted to any official or employee unless the previous cash advance given to him is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made to avoid misappropriation and/or loss of government funds.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Treasurer to intensify the collection of real property taxes and issue notice of delinquency as required in RA No. 7160 to all delinquent taxpayers for them to settle all their taxes due to augment income of the Municipality that could be used to finance priority programs and projects.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to immediately remit the unutilized balances to the Bureau of Treasury or return the unutilized funds to the source agencies in order for them to have additional funds for other priority projects and to finally end any obligation created by these transfers and to fully liquidate these accounts.

We recommended that the LCE:
Instruct the MPDO to formulate priority PPAs that are procurement-and-implementation-ready in order ensure its full implementation and optimal utilization of the 20% DF; and
Instruct the MPDO for the implementation of PPAs which are funded from the unutilized continuing appropriations of 20% DF and if these PPAs are no longer the priority programs of the Municipality, or no obligations arising therefrom exist and/ or savings from completed projects, propose to the Sanggunian to reprogram said funds for other priority PPAs thus, maximize implementation and utilization of the funds to uplift the economic and social well-being of the constituents.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the:
LDRRMO to incorporate in the Current Year’s LDRRMFIP the unexpended balance of LDRRMF from previous years to ensure the most possible available funds for disaster risk reduction, and
Municipal Accountant and LDRRMO to prepare and submit, respectively the prescribed format of the monthly Report on Utilization of DRRMF in order to provide complete information on the sources of the said fund and the expenditures for a particular month.

We recommended that the LCE:
instruct the Municipal Accountant, being one of the persons responsible in all the unsettled NS, to review the required documents stated in the NS and cause its immediate submission/ settlement; and
direct the Municipal Treasurer, in compliance with the directives of the issued COE, to cause the collection of the disallowances directly from the persons liable or withhold the payment of the salaries or any amount due them.

We recommended that the Management:
Thru the Inventory Committee, prepare and submit the RPCPPE, taking into consideration the capitalization threshold of P50,000.00, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and
Undertake all other procedures for the one-time cleansing of PPE balances to come up with the reconciled and correct balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements and submit the other documents as enumerated to determine compliance with COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to maintain complete subsidiary ledgers of the individual taxpayers to support the recorded balance of Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable to ensure the accuracy, validity and reliability of these accounts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Issue a demand letter requiring the retired OIC-Municipal Agriculturist to immediately account the animals distributed in the dispersal project and to submit the documents on the status thereto, otherwise, identify and hold persons liable for the value of the unaccounted animals;
Require the Municipal Agriculturist to comply with the appropriate procedures in the filing of a request for relief from property accountability to the Audit Team as provided in Section 73(1) of PD 1445 and Section 151, Rule 20, Title IV of the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management in the Local Governments; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to take up necessary accounting entries to recognize in the books of accounts the loss of Breeding Stocks and, where appropriate, to set up the corresponding receivables from concerned accountable officers, if the aforecited animals were not accounted and the request for relief from property accountability was not immediately filed with the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw the necessary journal entries to record the settlement of disallowance and other income to their appropriate accounts;
Require the Municipal Accountant to exert efforts in identifying the nature of the other long outstanding balances in the Other Payables account under the Trust Fund and to prepare adjusting journal entries to present these transactions to their proper accounts; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to record all transactions of the LGU to their appropriate accounts to ensure that the financial statements are fairly stated.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw the necessary journal entries to correct the balances of the “Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses” account and the affected permanent accounts; and
Advise the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all transactions are recorded in the correct accounts in the general ledger in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to exert efforts in identifying the cause of the unreconciled item and submit a copy to this Office of the adjustments to be made in the records of the LGU. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure the timely preparation and submission of monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements in compliance with Section 74 of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 for the timely evaluation thereof.

We recommended that the Management prepare a revised Annual Investment Plan incorporating the utilization of the prior years’ unexpended LDRRMF and specifying the list of PPAs to be charged against the fund prior to the utilization of the same in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 and NDRRMC-DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 to avoid suspensions or disallowances in audit.

We recommended that the Management utilize solely the LDRRM Fund to implement projects and activities under the legislated thematic areas namely: 1) disaster prevention and mitigation; 2) disaster preparedness; 3) disaster response; and 4) disaster rehabilitation and recovery, as provided in Section 21 of RA 10121 and Item 5.0 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013, to avoid suspensions or disallowances in audit.

We recommended the following to the Management:
To conduct a complete inventory count of all PPEs of the LGU and submit a Report of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment; and
To reconcile the inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 and undertake all other procedures prescribed under the same Circular to come up with the correct balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant effect the necessary correcting entries for the derecognition of semi-expendable items acquired and issued prior to CY 2024. We also recommended that the Management comply with the procedural guidelines provided in COA Circular 2022-04 and COA Circular No. 2024-06 on the treatment of semi-expendable items.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the transactions totaling P1,214,952.21 and ensure that the transactions are recorded in their appropriate accounts to accurately present the balances of the accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management:
Review and reconcile all transactions related to inter-local government unit transactions to ensure compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary correcting entries to reflect the correct account balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary correcting journal entries to properly record the payments for BAC Honoraria and record all subsequent disbursements for honoraria under the account “Honoraria”, to ensure the fair presentation of the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary correcting journal entries to reclassify the transactions under the Donations account and record all subsequent disbursements for financial assistance under the account Donations, to ensure the fair presentation of the Financial Statements.

We commended the Management with the good practice of timely liquidation of all cash advances and leaving no balance at the end of the year. We also recommend that the Management, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to continue the outstanding supervision to all of the AOs of the municipality to set a good example to other agencies.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the unliquidated fund transfers to Barangays as required under Sections 6.4 and 7.1 of COA Circular No. 2016-005;
Coordinate with the Barangays whether the projects are already implemented and require them to submit the liquidation reports, or if the projects are no longer feasible, to require them to return the funds; and
Put in place mechanisms that will facilitate the immediate implementation of the projects and to deter accumulation of unutilized municipal funds in the accounts of the barangays.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal Accountant:
Review the records of the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts and trace the errors leading to the unreconciled balances from CY 2016 up to CY 2024, and post the necessary adjustments to present the correct balances of the reciprocal accounts; and
Make a reconciliation of the reciprocal accounts every year-end to present an adjusted and reconciled balances in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that:
The Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the proper recognition of CIP accounts and PPE Account:
Moreover, regular monitoring and analysis of the Construction-in-Progress account shall be conducted to ensure the proper transfer of the completed projects to the appropriate asset accounts; and
The Municipal Engineer furnish the Municipal Accountant the report of completed projects together with the certificate of completion as a basis for the proper re-classification of CIP accounts to the proper PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal General Services Officer:
Prepare the IIRUP for the ten (10) defective/unserviceable PPE and submit the same to the Accounting Unit to be used as their basis in dropping from the books upon disposal;
Expedite the disposal of the defective/unserviceable properties to avoid further deterioration as prescribed under PD 1445 and the provisions of GAM Volume I and to present accurate valuation of the PPEs; and
Assign property tag/sticker for each type of PPE in compliance with Section 6.2.5 of COA Circular No. 2020-006; and
Coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to determine the costs of the PPE with no carrying amount and include the same in the RPCPPE.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant observe the use of appropriate liability accounts in the recording of obligations of the Municipality, as prescribed in the COA Circular No 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, to ensure fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management make proper written requests to the Source Agencies (SA) for authority to utilize the unused funds for projects or endeavors that are congruent to the PPAs of the SAs or to transfer the same to the General Fund so that these will be appropriated to accomplish the PPAs of the Municipality.  If such requests are disapproved, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer facilitate the immediate return of the unutilized balances to the SAs, so that these will be used by the national government for social services or other PPAs of the government.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain financial harmony and present the disclosures provided by section 54 of IPSAS 3 to ensure faithful representation of financial information in the financial statements which are very vital in decision making especially in observance of fiscal responsibility.

We recommended that:
The Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of charging and recording expenditures with specific accounts titles to Other MOOE Account, and charge/record the disbursements according to the nature of the expenditures, pursuant to the COA Circular above;
In case the allotted budget of certain specific expenditure account is insufficient, proper process of augmentation must be undertaken, and should not be directly charged to Other MOOE account; and
All department heads and other concerned personnel review their budget and their charges thereof that these lapses will not be repeated in the CY 2025.

We recommended that:
Municipal Accountant to:
prepare and maintain ELCs and record the repair history for each motor vehicle in accordance with the provisions of GAM Volume II; and
determine whether or not the repairs and maintenance on each vehicle already exceeded the 30 percent threshold pursuant to COA Circular No, 2012-003; and
General Services Officer to prepare and maintain a summary/report of repairs and maintenance per motor vehicle and submit the same to the Accounting Unit for reconciliation.

We recommended that Management formally request to the Sanguniang Bayan to promptly promulgate an ordinance updating the Schedule of Market Values (SMV) and undertake the general revision of real property assessments in compliance with Section 219 of Republic Act No. 7160 to generate additional funds to finance developmental projects and augment the special education fund.

We recommended that Management:
Stop purchasing office supplies through online platforms such as Shopee, Lazada and Tiktok, and follow the procedures provided under the Government Procurement Reform Act should there be an unforeseen contingency requiring immediate purchase, or the procurement of ordinary or regular office supplies and equipment from other suppliers if not available in the DBM-PS; and
Properly fill out the Purchase Orders by requiring the signature over printed name of the supplier or his representative as instructed under GAM Volume II for LGUs.

We recommend that Management communicate with the Contractor to rectify the defects detected during the inspection.  In case the Contractor refuse to do so, Management must instruct the Municipal Engineer to implement the repair and deduct the cost from the retention money due to the Contractor.  Thereafter, inform the Audit Team on the completion of the repairs made for further re-inspection.  Otherwise, submit a computation of the cost of the area with defects and these may be disallowed in audit.
We recommended further that the Municipal Engineer or his representative conduct timely evaluation of the completed projects before the acceptance of thereof after a year of its completion.  Should defects are found, these must be immediately communicated to the Contractor and require him to make the necessary corrections at his own expense.

We recommend that:
The Municipal Engineer  communicate with the Contractors to prepare and submit the SWA of the aforementioned projects at the expiration of their contract period, and to compute the liquidated damages that must be imposed to the Contractors.  Otherwise, they shall be held liable for the computed amount of liquidated damages incurred by these contractors due to their negligence.  Henceforth, require them to prepare the SWA of incomplete projects at the end of their contract period for the determination of number of days delay and imposition of liquidated damages to the defaulting contractors; and
The Municipal Engineer vigilantly monitor the implementation of the infrastructure projects of the Municipality and to impose liquidated damages to defaulting Contractors, pursuant to the provision of RA 9184.  Also, ensure that the grant of extension or suspension are really investigated and only those allowed under RA 9184 and its IRR and determined justifiable and acceptable are recommended for approval.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the LDRRM Officer adhere with the provisions of R.A. No. 10121 and COA Circular No. 2012-003 regarding the accounting and reporting of the transfer of unspent DRRM funds to the Trust Fund and the reversion of the balances still unutilized after five years to the General Fund which shall be available for appropriation for other social services.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal DRRM Officer continue their efforts to work hand in hand to properly account and maintain appropriate records and reports on the transfer and reversion of LDDRM funds to expedite fiscal Management, report generation and improve the transparency of the utilization of the funds.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive, being the primary responsible official of the Municipality, require the Municipal Accountant, Human Resources Department, and all other concerned personnel to submit the documents within the prescribed period as required under Section 122 of PD No. 1445 and Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-006.

1.12 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the (a) MA
to recognize in the books the online collections totaling ₱901,015.80, and derecognize
the entries for the cancelled check and the double-entry transaction, to ensure the fair
presentation of the affected cash accounts; and (b) MA and MT to continuously adopt
sound practices to further strengthen internal controls in the management and
reporting of cash accounts and AFs.

2.12 We recommended that the LCE require the (a) MA to (i) adhere to the PIM
guidelines for the recording and issuance of inventories, and (ii) prepare adjusting
entries for the unrecognized issued/consumed inventories that have already been
distributed to the intended recipients; and (b) GSO Officer to prepare the SSMI along
with the RIS and submit them to the MAO for the proper recording of issued
inventories in the books.

3.29 We recommended that the LCE require the (a) MA to: (i) prepare the
necessary adjustments to reclassify the asset items and related accumulated
depreciation to the appropriate accounts and thereafter, observe the ₱50,000.00
capitalization threshold when classifying/recording these tangible items as PPE; (ii)
draw necessary journal entry vouchers to reflect the aforementioned adjustments on
depreciation; (iii) recognize donated properties in the books of accounts; (iv)
derecognize transferred properties after verifying the completeness of supporting
documents; and (v) maintain and regularly update the PPELCs; (b) GSO to: (i)
maintain PCs for each type of property in coordination with the MAO; and (ii)
maintain LRIRM and LRNPC in coordination with the Municipal Engineer (ME); and (c)
ME to provide the MA and GSO with the complete description and cost segregation of
road components for road projects as basis for recording in the books of accounts.

4.8 We recommended that the LCE require the (a) Municipal officials concerned
to continuously ensure the prompt implementation of PPAs funded by NGAs; and (b)
MA to expedite the retrieval of pertinent records from previous years’ fund transfers
to facilitate the return of unexpended balances to the respective SAs.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to
direct the (a) Municipal Treasurer to (i) update the list of past due
RPT payers in order to report reliably the total delinquencies of the
Municipality as of report dates; and (ii) employ effective collection
strategies by updating and posting the notice of delinquency for
property taxes at designated areas and conduct massive campaign
and public information dissemination on the value of paying taxes;
and if warranted, enforce the provision of Section 254(b) and 256
of RA 7160 in the collection of delinquent taxes; and (b) Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records of
RPT/SET Receivables in order to report reliably the said accounts
in the financial statements.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant to refrain from granting additional cash advances to the
designated Special Disbursing Officer (SDO) unless the previous cash
advance given to her is first settled.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the (a) Municipal
Accountant to maintain SLC for all inventory accounts; and (b) GSO to
(i) conduct the required physical count of supplies and materials
semestrally and report the same in the RPCI for submission to the
Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year; and
(ii) maintain SC for each type of supplies to account for the receipt and
issuance of the same.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations and the LCE
agreed to require the (a) Inventory Committee and GSO to (i)
conduct a complete physical inventory of all PPE of the Municipality
on or before January 31 of each year, conclude said inventory count
for CY 2024 and immediately submit the complete RPCPPE to the
Audit Team; and (ii) reconcile the resultant inventory reports with
the accounting records; (b) GSO to update the PPE records such as
Property Cards for the proper control and monitoring of the
agency’s property and equipment, and submit the IIRUP for the
identified unserviceable assets; and (c) Municipal Accountant and
GSO to consider the guidelines and procedures on one-time
cleansing of PPE accounts to render a reliable PPE information.

We also recommended that the Disposal Committee facilitate the
disposal of the noted unserviceable PPEs in accordance with the
prescribed guidelines and procedures.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant to continue complying with Section 50 of the NGAS
Manual for LGUs, Volume I on (i) classifying and recording PPE as
CIP during the construction period and (ii) reclassifying completed
projects to appropriate PPE accounts.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and the LCE agreed to
direct the concerned officers to a) continue to comply with the
guidelines on the receipt, utilization and liquidation of all fund
transfers of the National Government Agencies; and b) remit the
long outstanding and idle fund balances to the Bureau of the
Treasury for inclusion in the unappropriated surplus of the
National Government and be available for new appropriation and
better use.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the concerned
officers to: (a) continue to prioritize the payment of terminal leave
benefits of employees who are separated from government service
to comply with RA No. 10154 and CSC MC No. 07, s. 2013; and (b)
recognize and make necessary adjustment in the Employee Benefits
account, update/reconcile employee leave credits and perform other
relevant activities within three (3) years from the effectivity of COA
Circular No. 2024-009.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the (a) BPLO to (i)
strictly implement and enforce the adjusted tax rates based on the
updated Municipal Revenue Code; (ii) ensure that the prescribed
rates are correctly applied based on the line of business of the
taxpayer; and (iii) require the business tax payers to submit a sworn
statement of gross sales/receipts for the initial basis of computation
of business tax and a copy of ITR for the proper computation of
deficiency tax, if any; (b) Municipal Treasurer to examine and
verify the books of accounts and other pertinent records of any
business establishments; and (c) BPLO and Acting Municipal
Treasurer to verify, assess, and validate other businesses aside from
the six sampled businesses and demand the payment of computed
under collection of business taxes amounting to ₱36,465.00.

We recommended and the Local Development Council (LDC), headed
by the LCE, agreed to continue to plan and effectively implement
priority developmental projects allowing to flow essential benefits to
constituents.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the LDRRMC,
through the LDRRM Officer, to continually identify LDRRMF
related PPAs to be undertaken during the budget year in order to
ensure their full implementation and attainment of the expected
outcomes within the targeted time frame. We further recommended
that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to transfer the
savings from implemented/ completed projects under Continuing
Appropriation to Special Trust Fund to be available for other
DRRM PPAs.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation and the LCE
agreed to direct the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), through the
GAD Focal Person, to (a) continue its efforts to improve gender
mainstreaming to achieve full compliance with the provisions of
relevant laws and regulations; and (b) ensure the complete
implementation of activities indicated in the GPB to maximize the
intervention in addressing gender-issues in the Municipality.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the Municipal
Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer continue to comply
with the BIR rules and regulations on the withholding and
remittance of taxes on compensation, value added taxes, percentage
taxes and expanded taxes from government money payments.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the Municipal
Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer continue to comply
with the R.A. No. 8291 or GSIS Act of 1997 in the withholding of
premiums and loan amortizations from employees’ compensation
together with the government's share and its timely remittances to
GSIS.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the Municipal
Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer continue to comply
with the R.A. No. 9679 or HDMF law of 2009 on the withholding of
contributions and loan amortizations from employees’ compensation
together with the government’s share and its timely remittances to
HDMF.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the Municipal
Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer continue to comply
with the R.A. No. 7875 as amended by R.A. Nos. 9241 and 10606 or
the National Health Insurance Act of 2013 on the withholding of
contributions from employees’ compensation together with the
government’s share and its timely remittances to PHIC.

We recommended and Management agreed that (i) the Accounting and Treasury Offices continue maintaining proper accounting of the Cash accounts and monitoring the book and bank reconciling items, and (ii) the Accounting Office consistently prepare and submit the monthly bank reconciliation statements.

We recommended and Management agreed that the (a) Municipal Treasurer (i) continue to furnish the updated RPTAR/ Taxpayer’s index card to Municipal Accountant at every beginning of the year for recording and adjustment purposes; and (ii) provide the Municipal Accountant with reports of collections of real property taxes showing also the gross amounts, specifically those with discounts; and (b) Municipal Accountant (i) align the accounting procedures being adopted with the procedures provided by the MNGAS for LGUs with respect to recording of RPT/SET transactions; and (ii) continue to establish RPT and SET receivables sourced from the list duly certified by the Treasury Office at the beginning of the year.

We recommended and the Accounting Office and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to (i) maintain the SCs, SPCs, SLPCs and SPLCs for all LGU inventories and observe the periodic reconciliation of the said records to monitor agency assets properly; (ii) conduct a complete semi-annual physical count of inventories and submit the corresponding RPCI not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year in accordance with Section 124 of the MNGAs for LGUs; (iii) maintain an SSMI and RSPI to serve as a basis for preparing Journal Entries to record the supplies and materials and semi-expendable properties issued and (iv) maintain a RegSPI to ensure proper monitoring of semi-expendable properties.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee, GSO and Municipal Accountant, as the case may be, agreed to: (i) make the necessary adjustments to correct all the aforementioned misstatements; (ii) align the accounting procedures being adopted with the procedures provided by COA Circulars Nos. 2020-006 and 2022-004; (iii) submit revised Local Road Asset Management Forms duly providing all the necessary information, as discussed; (iv) maintain an IIRUP to properly document and track the unserviceable assets of the Municipality; and (v) for PPE items without available records, follow the procedures provided under Section 8 of COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended and Management agreed that the (a) Municipal Accountant recognize leave benefits quarterly as they are earned, rather than recording the entire liability in a single year; (b) Municipal Budget Officer establish an Employee Benefits Reserve Fund to fund for all the leave benefits owed to employees; and (c) Municipal Human Resource Management Office to coordinate with the Municipal Accounting Office and Municipal Budget Office to ensure adherence to Rule XVI of the Omnibus Rules Implementing Book V of Executive Order (EO) No. 292 on the yearly accounting and funding of leave benefits.

We reiterated our recommendation and Management agreed that the officers and employees in charge of the implementation of projects funded by other agencies exert their best efforts in coordinating with SAs to determine whether the purposes of the fund transfers can still be achieved and warrant a refund, if necessary.

We recommended and Management agreed that the (a) Municipal Accountant book the 20% LGU share from Fire Code Revenues in the GF; and (b) Local Chief Executive (LCE) subject its utilization under the process of regular appropriation through the approval of local Sanggunian.

We recommended that the (a) Local Chief Executive (LCE) continue upholding its mandate in promoting local development by appropriating 20% of its annual NTA pursuant to the provisions of the DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 and JMC No. 01 dated November 04, 2020; (b) MEO together with the MPDO ensure the implementation of all PPAs within the target schedules; and (c) BAC together with the Technical Working Group (TWG), submit the lacking supporting documents, as discussed, and ensure the completeness of documentation before the processing of payment of claim/s by the contractor/s.

We recommended and the MDRRM Officer, in collaboration with MDRRMC agreed to (a) ensure that the use of LDRRMF is maximized; (b) identify and incorporate, under a separate caption in the LDRRMFIP the projects, programs, and activities that will be charged against the unexpended LDRRMF from previous years; and (c) update or revise the LDRRMFIP as necessary to effect any changes in the PPAs during the year.

We commended Management for the effective collection performance and reiterated our recommendation and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to (i) continue to comply and enforce the Local Revenue Code so that correct business tax will accrue for the benefit of the Municipality; (ii) require the submission of a sworn statement of the business’ gross sales/receipts and not just mere certifications from company officers, and copy of income tax return, to ensure that the basis declared by the business establishments are reliable and accurate; and (iii) examine the books of businesses to ascertain the correctness of assessment and collection of business tax.

We recommended and the GFPS agreed to (i) inform the concerned DILG Office in writing regarding the changes in the DILG-endorsed GPB in a timely manner to ensure that they have ample time to review if the actual results conformed with established performance indicator and target and (ii) strive for the full implementation of the identified and approved PPAs by utilizing judiciously the fund set aside for said purposes.

We recommended and Management agreed to (i) design a more effective strategy that will allow a timelier recording of transactions to facilitate the reconciliation between actual taxes withheld and remitted; (ii) ensure that personnel’s salaries are withheld of taxes whether processed through payroll or disbursement voucher; (iii) provide a separate schedule detailing the discrepancies between the monthly withheld and remitted amounts to have a clear and thorough understanding of the amounts posted in the books of accounts; and iv) continue to observe the rules and regulations governing the withholding and remittance of taxes to the BIR.

We reiterate our recommendation and Management agreed that the (a) HRMO and Accounting Office take the necessary measures to lessen or eliminate the over and/or under remittances of the balances of Due to GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and PhilHealth accounts; and (b) Municipal Accountant provide a separate schedule detailing the discrepancies between the monthly withheld and remitted amounts to have a clear and thorough understanding of the amounts posted in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Inventory Committee together
with the CGSO and City Accounting Office (CAO) to complete the procedural
guidelines for one-time cleansing of PPE balances, as prescribed in COA
Circular No. 2020-006, to eliminate the noted discrepancy amounting to
P1,558,650,529.16 as of December 31, 2024 in order to come up with accurate
and more reliable reports.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the
City Treasurer to trace the material variance of P2,137,596,603.89 between the
subsidiary ledgers and cashbook balances and draw Journal Entry Voucher/s
to effect the necessary adjustments to the affected accounts, if necessary.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer and the City
Accountant to exert utmost effort to identify the details of the reconciling items
with a net amount of P6,570,710.71 so that appropriate journal entries could be
drawn by the latter in the books.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to correctly
classify the outstanding balance of the long-term loans payable by presenting
the portion of loans due for settlement within 12 months in the amount of
P242,183,192.87 as part of the current liabilities and the remaining balance of
P2,646,079,542.13 which is due for settlement beyond one year as non-current
liabilities in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to reinstate the Accounts Payable by preparing the necessary adjusting JEV in accordance with Section 61 of the MNGAS for LGUs to properly reflect the outstanding obligation in the financial statements; and
b. Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Treasurer to issue written notices to payees at least one (1) month before a check becomes stale, in compliance with Section 59 of the MNGAS for LGUs, to minimize the occurrence of stale checks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Chairperson of the BAC to submit a written justification for the noted deficiencies. Non-compliance may result in the issuance of a Notice of Suspension or Disallowance, as warranted; and
b. Henceforth, instruct the Chairperson of the BAC to strictly adhere to Item 9.1 of DBM Budget Circular No. 2022-1 to ensure that future procurements comply with existing regulations and promote the efficient use of government resources.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer, together with the heads of the concerned LEEs, to:
a. Develop a sound financial plan by preparing the Annual Operating Budget (AOB) for LEE operations, integrating it into the local development plans, and ensuring its inclusion in the Annual Budget once approved;
b. Ensure compliance with DBM LBC No. 100 and LBC No. 111, particularly regarding the proper use of subsidies and revenue management; and
c. Implement financial sustainability measures to enable LEEs to cover their operating costs and reduce dependency on subsidies by:
i. Enhancing revenue generation through fee adjustments, optimized collections, and exploring additional income sources;
ii. Implementing cost control measures through regular expense reviews; and
iii. Monitoring financial performance and making necessary adjustments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to henceforth ensure that:
a. all claims paid through ATM are recorded directly under the appropriate PS or MOOE accounts, following applicable accounting and auditing standards; and
b. payments for the salaries and wages of regular and casual employees, as well as honoraria for services rendered by JO workers, are supported with complete documentation pursuant to Sections 4.1.3 and 4.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the HRMO to:
a. Inform and remind employees whose NTHP falls below the P5,000.00 threshold about the limits set under Section 56 of the General Provisions of the GAA for FY 2024;
b. Strictly monitor compliance with the mandated NTHP requirement when processing new or additional loan applications; and
c. Review and update the Municipality’s loan deduction policies to prevent recurrence of excessive deductions through coordination with lending institutions to establish a mechanism ensuring compliance with the NTHP threshold before processing loan deductions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Local Development Council (LDC) and the concerned municipal offices to ensure that PPAs proposed under the 20 percent DF are well-planned, procurement-ready, and implementation-ready, in accordance with Section 3.2.3 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1;
b. Require the MPDC to include a detailed implementation schedule in the AIP and ensure that any modifications are promptly reported to and approved by the appropriate authorities. A copy of the revised or final AIP should also be furnished to the Audit Team; and
c. Require the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that any adjustments in the Municipality’s actual share in the IRA or national taxes are appropriately reflected in the 20 percent DF through a Supplemental Budget, in compliance with Section 287 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) Executive Committee to:
a. Ensure the effective and efficient implementation of the GAD PPAs and the judicious utilization of the GAD Budget by:
i. Strengthening capacity development on gender mainstreaming, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, gender analysis, and gender assessment; and
ii. Updating the sex-disaggregated data contained in the GAD database to provide an accurate basis for planning and budgeting; and
b. Institutionalize a systematic feedback mechanism within the GAD Monitoring and Evaluation Team to ensure timely updates on the effectiveness of GAD Programs, PPAs and to prevent similar planning deficiencies.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to submit the required LDRRMFIP or LDRRMP and revised AIP, ensuring that both documents clearly present the PPAs implemented, along with the supplemental budget ordinance, to ensure compliance with Item 6.5 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all transactions charged against the unexpended LDRRMF balances are properly documented with complete supporting records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to revisit the procedural guidelines in inventory taking prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006 and ensure that the inventory taking is properly documented using the standard ICF in order to address the noted discrepancies and to arrive at an accurate and more reliable PPE balance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to prepare a Schedule of Unreleased Checks at the end of the year and submit it to the Municipal Accountant; and
b. Municipal Accountant, based on the report from the Municipal Treasurer, to prepare the JEV for the restoration of cash equivalent of the unreleased checks and recognize the appropriate payable/liability accounts and in addition, reverse the entries made in the ensuing year, in compliance with the pertinent provisions of COA GAFMIS Circular Letter No. 2002-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to trace the material variance of P2,067,478.51 between the subsidiary ledgers and cashbook balances and draw Journal Entry Voucher/s to effect the necessary adjustments to the affected account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the LDRRM Officer in identifying and addressing the cause/s of the discrepancy between the unutilized balance per Utilization Report and the amount reported in the Statement of Financial Position.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to exert effort to collect the subject receivables totaling P3,717,255.92. If these efforts prove futile, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the submission to the COA of the request for authority to write-off the dormant receivables together with the necessary documents enumerated in Section 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
b. Direct all concerned officials to institute measures, including the imposition of sanctions on negligent officials, to ensure that all receivables of the Municipal are collected and cash advances are liquidated on due dates.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official or employee without liquidating first his/her prior cash advances.

In addition, consider designating additional Disbursing Officers (Dos) to facilitate cash disbursement activities efficiently.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all accountable officers and employees to ensure liquidation of cash advances within the prescribed period is complied with as required by COA Circular 97-002.

Moreover, instruct the Municipal Accountant to propose specific and achievable measures to resolve delays in the liquidation of cash advances, which include, among others, the Municipal Accountant’s regular sending of demand letters to AOs with a reminder that their salaries could be withheld should they still fail to liquidate after demand.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that cash advances are granted only to bonded officials/employees and strictly adhere to the provisions under Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 and COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the aforementioned lacking documents to facilitate validation by the Audit Team to establish the validity, propriety and legality of such transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission of photos, lists of recipients, their respective addresses and the items given to them, among other information necessary to facilitate validation by the Audit Team and establish the propriety and validity of such transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that individual accomplishment reports and DTRs of JO personnel are attached in the liquidation reports of cash advances for wages of JO personnel to avoid audit suspension of their wages.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to cause the submission of the noted lacking documents to the COA Audit Team to facilitate further technical review of the COA Technical Audit Division in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001, otherwise, the transactions covered by the same will be suspended in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Require the Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to prepare and submit the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption to the Audit Team; and
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that disbursements for gasoline and fuel are supported with complete documentation in compliance with the pertinent provisions of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001 and COA Circular No. 77-61.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Treasurer to implement adequate internal control measure in the management of
collections and deposits by ensuring that all collections remitted by the collecting
officers are properly reported during the month these were actually received and
by conducting regular reconciliation of records to ensure the completeness and
accuracy of the reported balance of CLT account at year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to (i) regularly monitor and review the list
of outstanding checks, particularly those that have remained unclaimed or
uncashed for more than six months, and take appropriate action to address them;
and (ii) initiate communication with the concerned payees to determine the cause
of the delays in encashment and take corrective action where necessary, such as
reissuance of checks, when appropriate.

We reiterated the prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to make an extra effort to submit the
bank reconciliation statements on time.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to initiate the necessary actions required to avail of the one-time
cleansing of dormant accounts, as provided under COA Circular No. 2023-008 to
properly dispose these long outstanding undocumented cash advances and to
present the reliable balance of account in the Municipality’s financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor advise the
Municipal Accountant to send demand letters to enforce the collection of these
receivables or initiate appropriate action required under COA Circular No. 2023-
008 to avail of the one-time cleansing of dormant accounts.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a complete physical count of all
inventories; (ii) reconcile the results of the count with the accounting
records; and (iii) prepare and submit to the Audit Team the corresponding
inventory reports; and
b. Municipal Accountant and Supply Officer to exert extra effort to maintain and
regularly update the Supplies Ledger Cards and Stock Cards, respectively, for
each inventory item and periodically reconcile the two records.

We therefore recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct complete physical count of
all properties (excluding Semi-expendable Properties) annually; (ii) reconcile the
results of the count with the accounting records; and (iii) prepare and submit to
the Audit Team the corresponding inventory reports in accordance with Section
124, Volume I of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs; and
b. require the Municipal Accountant and Supply Officer to maintain and
regularly update the PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively, for
each item of PPE and periodically reconcile the two records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. coordinate with the former Municipal Accountant to trace the details of
undocumented dormant fund transfers, and to initiate the necessary
actions to avail of the one-time cleansing of dormant accounts pursuant to
COA Circular No. 2023-008; and
b. coordinate with the concerned source agencies to confirm the unliquidated
balances of fund transfers and facilitate the prompt return of said balances
in compliance with Item 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Acting Municipal Treasurer to strengthen the internal control in cash management
by: (a) requiring all the collecting officers to remit their collections intact and daily
to the Acting Municipal Treasurer regardless of the amount collected; (b) being
more circumspect in the acknowledgement of remittances in terms of accuracy of
the data and amount; and (c) preparing RCD for his collections and remittances
made by the collectors during the day.

We reiterated our recommendation with some revisions that the Municipal Mayor
require the:
a. Acting Municipal Treasurer to (i) ensure that the duly certified list showing the
name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year is submitted
to the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year pursuant to Section 20
of the MNGAS, Volume I; and (ii) adopt strategies (e.g., information campaign,
barangay caravan) to intensify the collection of real property tax and to
persuade taxpayers to take advantage of early payment discount. Firm support
of the local chief executive is necessary to encourage the local treasurers to
pursue collection by all means; and
b. Municipal Accountant to set up the RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of
the year based on the list submitted by the Treasurer’s Office.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider the reorganization of the
Inventory Committee by designating personnel with capacity to effectively perform
the job. Otherwise, require the incumbent Inventory Committee to: (a) attend
training or seminar on property and supply management or coordinate and ask
assistance from the Inventory Committees of other LGUs who have already
successfully conducted physical count; and (b) give time and importance to the
conduct of physical count of Inventories and PPEs despite of their busy schedule
or workload so that the corresponding RPCI and RPCPPE could be prepared and
submitted to the Audit Team within the timeframe.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor provide assistance to the
Acting Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant in the preparation of the
property and accounting records, respectively, by utilizing available job order
personnel, or if budget permits, hire job order personnel to assist the two offices.
Likewise, require said heads of offices to ensure that these separate but related
records are always reconciled.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require the
Municipal Assessor (a) together with the Municipal Accountant to complete and
submit to the Audit Team the noted lacking documents in compliance with Section
4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 and Item 13.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (b) to
register before the Registry of Deeds the acquired parcel of land in order to secure
a TCT in the name of the Municipality.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
instruct the:
a. members of the Municipal Development Council to ensure that all the
development projects to be funded by the 20% DF are well-planned and
procurement-and-implementation ready pursuant to Item 3.2.3 of DBM-
DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 to ensure the optimal utilization of the fund;
b. Municipal Engineer to (i) conduct the necessary detailed engineering
studies on the project Rehabilitation of Municipal Tourism Building,
taking into account concerns about structural capability and stability of the
existing structure and present them to the management for approval in order to
commence project implementation; and (ii) being the end user, provide in the
PPMPs the time schedule of implementation of all projects charged to 20% DF
for integration in the APP; and
c. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to (i) indicate the time schedule for
each identified procurement activity for all projects in the APP to efficiently
manage the conduct of the procurement transactions; and (ii) together with the
Municipal Engineer, fast track the implementation of the unimplemented
developmental projects both under the current and continuing appropriations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LDRRMO to (a)
ensure that the list of projects and activities funded by the additional LDRRMF
under supplemental budgets is incorporated in the revised/supplemental
LDRRMFIP for proper integration in the supplemental AIP, pursuant to Item
6.2 of the NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1; and (b) see to it that the
priority projects and activities identified in the LDRRMFIP are promptly
implemented to achieve the objectives of the Fund.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. members of the GFPS to formulate the GAD Agenda and furnish a copy to the
Audit Team; and
b. GAD Focal Person to (i) review/recheck the computation of the scores reflected
in the HGDG Checklists to ensure the correctness of the scores and the
attributed amount reported in the GPB; and (ii) submit to the Audit Team the
GAD AR within the period prescribed in Item V of Circular No. 2014-001.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. HRMO - Designee to indicate in the job order contract the specific function/s
and/or job description/s of the job order workers hired and ensure that said
contract is acknowledged by the personnel hired; and
b. Municipal Accountant to ensure that payments of wages and traveling expenses
to JOs/COS personnel are supported with complete and proper documentation
pursuant to Item 1.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Item B.5 of COA
Resolution No. 2021-044.

1.12 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure:
a. The Inventory Committee prepares and submits the RPCPPE using the
prescribed format, and in coordination with the Municipal Accountant
and the GSO, reconciles it with the accounting and property records,
and also considers performing the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts
under COA Circular No. 2020-006 to address discrepancies and
inaccuracies in PPE records;
b. The Municipal Accountant, GSO and concerned offices trace and
document the origins of PPE items with lump-sum balances or missing
documentation, compute and record for the related depreciation, and
take appropriate corrective actions to ensure the accuracy and
reliability of the Municipality’s financial records;
c. The Municipal Accountant computes the unrecorded depreciation for
the PPE item classified under the Other Structures account, reconciles
the variances in accumulated depreciation for the Information and
Communication Technology Equipment and Furniture and Fixtures
accounts, and implements the necessary adjusting entries;
d. The Municipal Accountant and the GSO comply with the recognition,
documentation, and reporting requirements for tangible items falling
below the ₱50,000.00 capitalization threshold, in accordance with the
provisions of COA Circular No. 2022-004;
e. The Municipal Accountant reviews all PPE acquisitions, recomputes
depreciation starting from the date the assets became available for use,
prepares the necessary journal entries to reflect the adjustments, and
ensures that depreciation is properly recorded in the books going
forward; and
f. The Municipal Accountant corrects the depreciation entries recorded
for the Five-Storey Municipal Hall Building by reversing the ₱1.637
million accumulated depreciation and adjusting the Government
Equity and Asset accounts accordingly.

2.11 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure the
Municipal Accountant reviews the identified recording errors and
implements the necessary corrections to ensure the accuracy and
reliability of the Municipality’s financial records.

3.7 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure the
Municipal Accountant reverts the unutilized balance of the DRRMF 2019
under the Special Trust Fund, to the unappropriated surplus of the
General Fund, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-002, and
strictly complies with the proper accounting and reversion of disaster
funds moving forward.

4.9 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure the
Municipal Treasurer verifies the status of the identified stale checks and
submits the necessary documents to the Municipal Accountant, who shall
then prepare the appropriate Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to record the
required adjustments.

5.7 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure the
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer immediately submit the
disbursement vouchers, supporting documents, and cancelled checks to the
Audit Team, and that they strictly comply with the prescribed timeframe
for the submission of accounts moving forward.

6.10 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure:
a. The Municipal Treasurer prepares the certified list of RPT and SET
due and collectible based on the Real Property Tax Account Register
(RPTAR) or Index Card from the Assessor’s Office and submits the
report to the Municipal Accountant for the setting up of the
receivables; and
b. The Municipal Accountant recognizes in the books the RPT and SET
Receivables at the beginning of the year based solely on the certified
list of due and collectible real property taxes for the current year as
submitted by the Municipal Treasurer.

7.9 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure the
Municipal Treasurer actively monitors real property tax collections,
implements more effective collection strategies to enhance efficiency, and
enforces the remedies outlined in Sections 256 to 260 of RA No. 7160 for
delinquent RPT/SET.

8.10 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure the Municipal
Assessor:
a. Exhausts all efforts to gather the supporting documents necessary for
the titling of the lands owned by the LGU; and
b. Expedites the titling of all parcels of land owned by the LGU in
compliance with Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 and furnish
the Municipal Accountant of the supporting documents for recording
purposes.

8.11 We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure the
Municipal Accountant:
a. Makes the necessary adjustments or corrections for the unidentified
land recorded in the books, if warranted; and
b. Record in the books the land owned by the Municipality upon receipt
of the land titles, following Section 72 and of the MNGAS for LGUs
and IPSAS 45.

9.14 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure:
a. The GSO Designate and the Municipal Accountant comply with the
reporting requirements on inventory as prescribed in the MNGAS for
LGUs Volume I; and
b. The Inventory Committee conducts a physical inventory to validate
the ₱4.593 million beginning balance, the Municipal Accountant
reconciles it with accounting records, prepares necessary adjustments,
and, if warranted, requests its write-off in line with COA Circular No.
2023-008 on the one-time cleansing of covered dormant accounts.

9.15 We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure the
Municipal Accountant prepares the JEV to adjust the erroneous recording
of office supplies issued.

10.13 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure:
a. The Municipal Accountant expedites the reconciliation of the identified
accounts, prepare a detailed schedule of unreconciled balances with
supporting documentation, and make the necessary adjusting entries to
reflect the correct year-end balances of the Due to BIR, GSIS,
PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG accounts; and
b. The Municipal Accountant designs and implements an internal control
and monitoring system to ensure that discrepancies are identified,
investigated, and resolved in a timely manner, thereby maintaining the
accuracy of account balances.

11.15 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure:
a. The Municipal Development Council, MPDO and Municipal Engineer,
as the primary implementing offices discuss, prepare, and adhere to a
timetable for project implementation of the unimplemented projects to
ensure timely completion;
b. The MPDO makes certain that all programs and projects are
procurement- and implementation-ready, in compliance with DBM-
DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, to guarantee on-time execution;
c. The Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal
Engineer reconcile their reports regularly and establish the correct
utilization and balances of the current and continuing appropriations to
ensure the production of accurate year-end reports;
d. The BAC standardizes and aligns the delivery terms across the
Invitation to Bid, Purchase Order, and Notice to Proceed and enforces
tracking of Purchase Order receipt dates to ensure clarity in contract
implementation periods and compliance with delivery timelines;
e. The Municipal Engineer requires all pakyaw or service contracts to
clearly indicate the project start and completion dates, and update
standard contract templates accordingly, to ensure proper monitoring
and evaluation of contractor compliance with implementation
schedules; and
f. The BAC and Municipal Accountant strictly enforce compliance with
Section 62.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and GPPB
Resolution No. 30-2017 by requiring warranty security as a
prerequisite for payment to protect the Municipality’s right to claim
against post-acceptance manufacturing defects.

12.13 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure:
a. The MDRRM Council reassess all PPAs included in the LDRRMFIP that
remained unimplemented to date and determine their necessity,
otherwise, reprogram to priority PPAs and ensure its full
implementation in accordance with the requirements of the NDRRMC-
DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 to strengthen the Municipality’s disaster
risk reduction and management capability;
b. The Municipal Accountant makes certain that the Trust Liability –
DRRM balances under the Special Trust Fund are accurately reflected
in the Notes to Financial Statements;
c. The Municipal Engineer, in coordination with the Municipal
Accountant, properly identifies and accounts for fuel costs related to
project implementation in the Program of Works and in the books of
accounts;
d. The BAC standardizes and aligns the delivery terms across the
Invitation to Bid, Purchase Order, and Notice to Proceed and enforces
tracking of Purchase Order receipt dates to ensure clarity in contract
implementation periods and compliance with delivery timelines;
e. The Municipal Engineer requires all pakyaw or service contracts to
clearly indicate the project start and completion dates, and update
standard contract templates accordingly, to ensure proper monitoring
and evaluation of contractor compliance with implementation
schedules; and
f. The BAC and Municipal Accountant strictly enforce compliance with
Section 62.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and GPPB
Resolution No. 30-2017 by requiring warranty security as a
prerequisite for payment to protect the Municipality’s right to claim
against post-acceptance manufacturing defects.

13.10 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure:
a. The Municipal Accountant and concerned offices make certain that
funds received are supported with duly signed MOA and related
documents, and trust receipts are timely implemented and used solely
for their intended purpose;
b. The BAC standardizes and aligns the delivery terms across the
Invitation to Bid, Purchase Order, and Notice to Proceed and enforces
tracking of Purchase Order receipt dates to ensure clarity in contract
implementation periods and compliance with delivery timelines; and
c. The Municipal Engineer requires all pakyaw or service contracts to
clearly indicate the project start and completion dates, and update
standard contract templates accordingly, to ensure proper monitoring
and evaluation of contractor compliance with implementation
schedules.

14.11 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure:
a. The Municipal Accountant maintains separate books of accounts,
prepares periodic financial statements for each LEE, and furnishes
copies of these reports to the OIC-MPUEE and other concerned offices
to facilitate accurate assessment of each unit’s financial viability in
compliance with DBM LBC No. 100;
b. The OIC-MPUEE and concerned offices conduct the mandated
Financial Self-Sufficiency Review for each LEE to determine whether
they are progressing toward full cost recovery or financial self-
sufficiency. For LEEs found to be no longer self-sustaining, the
appropriate procedures prescribed under Item 7 of DBM LBC No. 111
should be followed;
c. The MPUEEO and the Municipal Accountant prioritize the full
repayment of all advances from the General Fund before any income
from LEE operations is absorbed by the General Fund, in accordance
with DBM LBC No. 100; and
d. The Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the MPUEEO Designate
and relevant offices, classify collections by individual LEE to enable
accurate evaluation of each unit’s revenue performance and overall
financial contribution.

15.12 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure:
a. The OIC – MPUEE makes certain that all market stall tenants have
valid and binding lease contracts; and
b. The OIC – MPUEE enforces the terms of the lease contracts for all
market stall tenants, particularly the provisions on sanctions for
unpaid rental fees.

16.8 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure the
Municipal Accountant prepares separate subsidiary ledgers for each fund
transfer, prepares the Fund Utilization Report accurately reflecting all
fund transfers received and utilized during the year, and submits the
report to the Office of the Auditor on time.

17.8 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure:
a. The HRMO maintains a complete and accurate manpower database to
properly track all JO and Casual personnel;
b. The concerned offices issue internal guidelines on wage rate increases
to justify and ensure their equitable and consistent application across
all personnel; and
c. The HRMO makes certain that all JO contracts clearly define the scope
of work and prescribe wage rates on a daily or hourly basis, in
accordance with Section 1 of CSC Resolution No. 021480.

18.10 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned
offices to fill the vacant LDRRMO II position, and create and fill the two
additional mandatory DRRM staff positions, in accordance with the
approved LDRRM staffing series prescribed in JMC No. 2014-1, if
warranted.

19.9 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure the
LDRRM Officer:
a. Submits monthly to the Auditor the Report on the Utilization of the
DRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month in
accordance with Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated
September 12, 2012; and
b. Furnishes the OCD and the DILG-OO a copy of the Monthly Report on
Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, in compliance with Section 5.1.6 of
COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

20.14 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure the
GAD Focal Point System through the GAD Focal Person submits the
