required supporting expenditure schedules for attributed organization and
client focused PPAs, and that future attributed costs in the GAD
Accomplishment Report are computed correctly based on actual
expenditures, to ensure that reported accomplishments are properly
identified, justified, and verifiable.

21.9 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure the
Local School Board:
a. Continues to provide the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal
Accountant, and Municipal Treasurer with a copy of the SEF Budget
for use in disbursements, fund certification and recording, no later
than seven (7) days after its approval;
b. Submits quarterly and annual reports on SEF utilization, using SEF
Budget Accountability Form No. 1 (Annex B) as prepared by the
Municipal Accountant, to the DepEd CO through appropriate
channels, copy furnished the local sanggunian concerned and
Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG, not later than the 20th day
after the end of the quarter for the quarterly reports, and not later
than the 15th day of February of the following year for the annual
reports; and
c. Posts the utilization report of the SEF Budget in the website of the
LGU and/or in at least three (3) conspicuous public places for
transparency and accountability in compliance with the Full
Disclosure Policy of the DILG.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Set-up the allowance for impairment on the unserviceable properties consistent with the requirements of IPSAS 21 to present fairly the PPE account in the financial statements; and
Prepare the proposed adjusting journal entry (PAJE) on the PPEs with negative carrying amounts, as shown below:
We also recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to create a Disposal Committee in accordance with the Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties.
We further recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed, that after creation of the Disposal Committee, the Committee undertake the inspection activities/processes, the appraisal procedures and valuation, the determination of appropriate mode of disposal and recommend the same for approval and the disposal proceedings in accordance with the Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to conduct complete physical count of inventory of supplies for semestral cutoffs June 30 and December 31 of every year, render the RPCI to the Audit Team in the form and deadline as required in Section 124 of the Manual and to reconcile the discrepancies.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to jointly reconcile the Subsidiary Ledger and Cashbook balances as of December 31, 2024, of the CIB- LCCA in the General and Trust Funds.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Record the following proposed journal entry to adjust the advance recording of liquidation of the CA for CF:
Henceforth, record the CA for CF, using the Advances to Special Disbursing Officers Account and the liquidation thereof, within 5 days after receipt of transmittal letter from ICFAU, as required by the JC.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Record the following proposed journal entry to adjust the overstatement of the Electricity Expense in CY 2024:
Henceforth, adopt guidelines on recording of expenses and require all departments to submit valid billings as of year-end and record as payables all unpaid obligations at year-end to avoid charging and recording of expenses in the ensuing year.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to review and analyze the accounts with negative balances and trace or identify the cause of the negative balances and effect the necessary adjustments to reflect the accurate balances of affected accounts to fairly present the financial statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Disclose the nature of prior period errors in the Notes to Financial Statements, in accordance with IPSAS 3; and
Present in the Statement of Changes in Net Assets/Equity each item of revenue and expense for the period that is recognized directly in net assets/equity, and the total of these items, in accordance with IPSAS 1.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor and other concerned officials/ employees agreed to:
Expedite the implementation of all pending development projects for the benefit of the constituents;
Assess the feasibility of the unimplemented Continuing projects, if determined to be not feasible, the funds should be reprogrammed to other viable and advantageous projects; and
Conduct proper planning of projects to be funded out of the 20% DF during budget preparation, so that projects are stated in specific terms, for faster implementation.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Maintain the Trust Liability-DRRM SLs for unutilized balances by year of transfer and receipts of transfers/grants from other LGUs by LGU; and
Transfer the recording of the transfers/grants from other LGUs amounting to ₱115,050.12 from the Due to LGUs account to the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account.

We recommended and the Municipality’s GFPS and LPDO agreed to, (a) institutionalize the GAD database with adequate and systematically gathered sex- disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation; (b) undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues and ensure that the PPAs are responsive to the said issues; and (c) revisit Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM- NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, which identifies the data requirements for gender issues.
We further recommended and the GFPS and TWG agreed to monitor the GAD Budget in order to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds through the implementation of GAD- related PPAs, to attain the objective for which funds were provided.
Lastly, we recommended and the GFPS agreed to adhere to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014, for the timely submission of the GAD AR to the COA Audit Team.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Reconcile, analyze and identify the cause/s of the negative balances of the Due to BIR subsidiary accounts and effect the necessary adjustments; and
Ensure proper recording of taxes withheld and remittances to the SL of the employees and suppliers, to establish their correct balances for a fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the Acting-Municipal Treasurer/Property Officer to fast track the conduct the physical inventory of all inventory items as well as the semi-expendable property in accordance with existing rules and regulations to facilitate the recording of the correct inventory account balances in the financial statements and submit the required report thereon to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively; and
direct the Acting-Municipal Treasurer/Property Officer and Municipal Accountant to maintain the required stock and ledger cards, respectively for inventory items and for semi-expendable property. Accordingly, ensure that the reported balances from these cards are reconciled thereon.
During the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant informed that she will utilize   the ECPAC System in the maintenance of the subsidiary ledgers per inventory items in lieu of the stock ledger cards.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Municipal Treasurer to prepare a duly certified list showing the names of the taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year which data should come from the RPTAR or Taxpayer’s Index Card maintained at the Municipal Treasurer’s Office;
Municipal Treasurer’s Office (MTO) to furnish a copy of the certified list to serve as basis in recording the RPT Receivable and SET Receivable accounts in the books; and
Municipal Accountant to record the receivables based on the list furnished by the Municipal Treasurer in order to recognize in the books an accurate and verifiable data pursuant to Section 19 and 20 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.

The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Mayor, in a letter reply, informed that the RPT and SET Receivables set up for the CY 2025 is based on a certification from the Office of the Municipal Treasurer for both RPT and SET Receivables.
The letter reply which was supported with a certification providing the amount of RPT Receivables for CY 2025 signed by the Acting Municipal Treasurer, but this was not supported with the certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year. Thus, the Audit Team will stand with the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the Acting-Municipal Treasurer submit to the Municipal Accountant the correct amount of delinquent taxes due as of December 31, 2023 and for the years thereafter so that updating and reconciling in the accountant’s record could be timely done.
Also, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant adjust the books of account to reflect the correct receivables and deferral accounts with the following proposed adjusting journal entry:
Deferred Real Property Tax 	₱472,245.90
Deferred Special Education Tax        472,245.90
Real Property Tax Receivable		  ₱472,245.90
Special Education Tax Receivable		  472,245.90
To adjust delinquent taxes due which were overstated by ₱472,245.90
In a letter reply, the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Mayor informed that at the start of the year 2024, setting up of the RPT Receivable and SET Receivable were based on amounts provided by the MTO through a certification because there was no other option. This practice might be avoided if the MTO and Municipal Assessor will go digital, which is currently in progress.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary using the following proposed adjusting journal entry:
Henceforth, adhere to COA Circular No. 2012-002 relative to the accounting and reporting of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to adjust the books of accounts to reflect the correct balance of the affected assets, expenses and equity accounts with the following proposed adjusting journal entry:
Prior Period Adjustment					₱   56,208.68
Depreciation - Infrastructure Assets		 1,545,859.22
Accumulated Depreciation – Road Networks	           ₱495,397.73
Accumulated Depreciation – Water Supply System           210,691.31
Accumulated Depreciation – Buildings                                434,870.78
Accumulated Depreciation-Other Infrastructure Asset      224,989.49
Accumulated Depreciation – Flood Control System             78,671.09
Accumulated Depreciation – Other Structures                    157,447.50
Further, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to record the infrastructure assets on the date when these assets are completed based on the certificate of completion, not in the last month of the year.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Stop the practice of outright recording to debit the Due to BIR account relative to the actual fund transfer from one fund to another fund and adjustments at year-end unless there is an actual fund transfer;
Submit the schedules to substantiate the adjustments made under JEV 100-24-12-07513, dated December 31, 2024; and
Submit reconciliation statement and analysis under Due to BIR account relative to the noted deficiencies and henceforth, record the fund transfers for the taxes due withheld in SEF and TF using the proforma entries as shown above.

We recommended and Management agreed, through the Municipal Accountant:
Conduct a thorough review of the Due to GSIS account and ensure the prompt remittance of the premiums and loan repayments in arrears amounting to ₱32,347.89, as of December 31, 2024;
Ensure that the amount withheld were remitted in full within the deadline pursuant to Section 6(b) and 7 of RA No. 8291.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to reclassify the payment for the vehicle insurance renewal with the following proposed adjusting journal entry:
Prepaid Insurance 			₱81,914.04
Due to GSIS				₱81,914.04
To reclass the erroneous entry made.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Implement the CY 2024 PhilHealth premium contribution schedule and adjust the employees and employers’ shares of the Due to PhilHealth account accordingly;
Make the necessary adjustments for employees with a withholding rate of 2.20 per cent, and subsequently deduct in the payroll the difference from the employees’ shares;
Trace the causes of the under-remittance to be able to make necessary actions such as immediate remittance of accounts, refund to the employees/personnel or adjustments in the books of accounts, whichever is applicable; and
Record the employer’s contribution for February 2024 and correct the erroneous HDMF contributions for JO employees with the following proposed adjusting journal entry:
Prior Period Adjustment 		₱45,236.13
Due to PhilHealth				₱45,236.13
To record the employer’s share of PhilHealth contribution for the month of February 2024.
Due to PhilHealth			₱3,753.05
Due to Pag-IBIG				₱3,753.05
To adjust the HDMF contributions of JO employees recorded under the Due to PhilHealth account under JEV No. 100-2024-02-04477 dated February 15, 2024.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Adhere to the recognition criteria and the use of the appropriate accounts in recording the payment of salaries and wages of JO personnel pursuant to prescribed Chart of Accounts for LGUs; and
Ensure that the payment of salaries and wages of JOs is supported with duly verified accomplishment reports.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant take definite action on the following:
Regularly reconcile the cashbook balances with the accounting records in order to timely effect the necessary corrections and consistently harmonize the balances of both records; and
Adjust cashbook records of the CIB- LCCA in the GF, SEF and TF as provided in the Bank Reconciliation Statement as at December 31, 2024 in order to establish the correct accountability.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to release the RPT share to Barangays, on a quarterly basis, pursuant to Section 271 (d) of RA No. 7160 in order for the latter to utilize the funds for its priority projects.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned officials make representation with the Provincial Government of Cebu to seek authority to realign the excess/unspent balances of the fully implemented projects.  Likewise, assess the feasibility/necessity of the projects which remained unimplemented, otherwise return the funds to the source agency.

We reiterated our audit recommendation and the Municipal Accountant agreed to trace the causes of the variances of the account to be able to make necessary actions such as immediate remittance of accounts, refund to the employees/personnel or adjustments in the books of accounts, whichever is applicable.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Submit a written justification on the approval of checks where the payee is in the name of the PBs instead of the name of the Barangay, as payee;
Through the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Acting-Municipal Treasurer, require the recipient Barangays to issue ORs as basic supporting documents and submit the same to Audit Team for evaluation; and
Require the Acting-Municipal Treasurer to issue the check directly to the creditor to whom the amount is due or to the LGU or LCE or Treasurer of the LGU pursuant to Section 57(F) of the LTOM dated October 1, 2007.

We reiterated our audit recommendation and the Municipal Accountant agreed to trace the variance of the taxes withheld and subsequent remittances so that appropriate actions shall be taken which is either additional remittance to the Bureau of Internal Revenue, or filing of tax credits for the refund of the over-remittances, and/or effect adjustments in the books of accounts, whichever is applicable.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the
Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Assessor, agreed to reconcile the
discrepancies and ensure accurate recording of RPT and SET Receivables,
taking into account the effects of the implementation of the RPVAR Act.

We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the
necessary adjusting entries to accurately reflect the correct amounts of RPT
and SET receivables as of December 31, 2024.

Lastly, we recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to, henceforth,
ensure the timely submission of the Certified List of Taxpayers, the
corresponding amount due and collectibles during the year, and the list of
delinquent taxpayers, so the Municipal Accountant can record this
information in the books of the Municipality.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to, henceforth,
include the required disclosures for the Road Network account as required
under COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended and Management agreed to prioritize the completion of the
one-time PPE cleansing in full compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-006
by assigning dedicated personnel and temporarily relieving the members of
the Inventory Committee from their regular duties to ensure the timely
execution of all required procedures and to immediately reconcile the results
of the physical count with accounting and property records.

We further recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to file a request
for authority to derecognize non-existing/missing PPEs to the audit team, and
support its request with the certified lists required in Section 8.3 of COA
Circular No. 2020-006, to take full advantage of the one-time cleansing
privilege afforded by the Commission and establish accuracy of its PPE
balances reported in the financial statements.

Lastly, we recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to adopt the
uniform numbering system for property numbers of PPE and require the
Property Unit to update the property stickers based on the prescribed format
and to include the required information under Sections 5.6 and 5.7 of the said
COA Circular.

We recommended and Management agreed to install internal control
mechanisms by using the RIS to request supplies and materials from stock
and to consolidate them in the SSMI, which shall be submitted to the
Municipal Accountant for recording in the appropriate expense accounts for
the supplies consumed.

We further recommended and Management agreed to establish a secure and
appropriate storage facility for supplies and materials to facilitate proper
inventory management and control.

Lastly, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to stop the
practice of recording purchases of supplies and materials directly to expense
accounts and henceforth, record purchases and utilization of supplies and
materials in accordance with Section 114 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs,
Volume I and Section IV of COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014,
to fairly present the expense and inventory accounts in the financial
statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
5.8.1 Make the necessary adjustments to correct the erroneous recording of
prior period expenditures to reflect the actual financial performance of
the Municipal Government of Sta. Catalina, in conformity with Section
119 of PD 1445 and IPSAS 1; and
5.8.2 Direct the Accounting Clerk assigned to record the expenditures to,
henceforth, review those that pertain to the prior period and journalize
these items as adjustments to equity and corresponding real or accrued
accounts rather than to current period expenditures or nominal accounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to establish internal
accounting policies to identify whether a particular expenditure pertains to
Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses, Training Expenses, or
Extraordinary and Miscellaneous Expenses. We also recommended and the
Municipal Accountant agreed to, henceforth, strictly follow the Revised Chart
of Accounts, as required in COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to ensure the timely
consolidation and submission of the SSMI in compliance with Sec. 121 of the
NGAS Manual for LGUs, Vol. 1.

We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to conduct a
thorough reconciliation of inventory balances and record the corresponding
journal entries to reflect the correct financial position.

We recommended and Management agreed to install internal control
mechanisms by using the ICS to request supplies and materials from stock
and to consolidate the same in the RSPI, which shall be submitted to the
Municipal Accountant for recording in the appropriate expense accounts for
the supplies consumed.

We further recommended and the Accountable Officer, Office of the
Municipal Treasurer, and Office of the Municipal Accountant agreed to
prepare the required forms, reports, and registries as prescribed in COA
Circular No. 2024-006.

Lastly, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to,
henceforth, adhere to the guidelines prescribed in COA Circular No. 2024-
006 and make the appropriate entries for semi-expendable items.

We recommended and the Municipal Engineer agreed to evaluate the
feasibility and closely monitor the implementation of the various PPAs
according to the approved development plan as required under Section 476
(4) of R.A. No. 7160. In addition, we recommended and Management agreed
to consider all possible limitations, including the availability of equipment,
during the planning stage.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget
Officer agreed to reconcile their reports and determine the actual funds
available for ongoing and/or unimplemented projects under the 20 percent
DF. We further recommend that Management, upon the authorization of the
Sanggunian, open a separate bank account in the General Fund for the 20
percent DF, to which the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer
transfer the determined or reconciled balance of the said account.

We recommended and the BAC agreed to evaluate and address bottlenecks
causing delays in the procurement process, including the posting of NOAs
and NTPs, to enhance transparency in government procurement, in
accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9184, as amended by RA No. 12009.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to develop an
effective tax collection campaign to enhance the collection of RPT/SET
Receivables and enforce remedies in the collection of real property such as
the issuance of a warrant on or before, or simultaneously with, the
institution of civil action for the collection of the delinquent tax.

We recommended and Management agreed to, henceforth, secure the
Certificate of Availability of Funds (CAF) from the Municipal Accountant,
prior to entering into any contract and before making any disbursement or
obligations charged to any authorized appropriation, in accordance with
Section 86 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended and Management agreed to desist from granting
incentives to enforcers and evaluate the legality and validity of Sec. 26 of
Res. No. 2008-057, regarding the grant of 25 percent incentives to enforcers,
regardless of employment status, vis-à-vis Sec. 12 of RA 6758 and Sec. 45 of
RA 9003, as amended.

We further recommended and the SWM Board agreed to:
14.13.1 Review and revise the current incentive allocation to ensure alignment
with the provisions of RA 9003;
14.13.2 Establish clear guidelines and/ or criteria for providing incentives and
awarding of rewards and recognitions; and
14.13.3 Prioritize rewarding LGUs, enterprises, and private entities for
implementing outstanding and innovative projects aimed at
developing effective SWM systems.

We recommended and the Municipality’s Solid Waste Management Board
agreed to:
15.10.1 Conduct a comprehensive review of its existing solid waste
management ordinance to ensure alignment with the provisions of RA
9003 and 11898; and
15.10.2 Enhance enforcement strategies, to ensure that penalties are
consistently applied, discouraging violations and improving overall
waste management practices.

We further recommended and the MENRO agreed to conduct public
consultations and develop awareness campaigns to inform residents and
businesses about the changes in fines, penalties, and their responsibilities
under the updated ordinance.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to prepare the
RPCRNS and reconcile it with the records available in the Office of the
Accountant. We also recommend that the GSO submit the Report on LRN as
required under COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the General Services Officer or the official responsible in accepting deliveries to furnish a notice to the Office of the Auditor on the deliveries of procured items within 24 hours from the acceptance to comply with the requirement of COA Circular No. 96-010. If personal submission of said notice is not possible, the duly signed scanned copy of the Inspection and Acceptance Report could be submitted thru the official email address of the Audit Team (lgsagbteam07@yahoo.com) to enable the latter to conduct inspection of procured items/goods to determine whether or not such deliveries conformed with the quantity, quality and specifications in the Purchase Order.  Thus, in case of any discrepancies, Management be immediately informed for its appropriate and immediate action. Likewise, it is encouraged and the LCE agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant and the latter assured to require the pictures of the items/goods delivered to be attached in the Disbursement Voucher.
observations.managementAction: Deliveries of procured items were notified to the Office of the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer and the latter assured to impose internal rules in the movement or flow of transactions, like the claiming of checks, in order to expedite submission of reports and documents to the Municipal Accountant for submission of the latter to the Auditor. Likewise, we recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant and the latter committed to strictly observe the timely and complete submission of accounts, in order to facilitate immediate review, evaluation and examination of consummated transactions, thus financial/ operational deficiencies, if any, could be communicated to Management for its immediate and appropriate action.
observations.managementAction:
Internal rules in the movement or flow of transactions were imposed to expedite submission of reports and documents.

The Municipal Accountant was able to submit accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the Inventory Committee and the latter promised to conduct annually complete physical count of all PPE and report the result of inventory in the RPCPPE and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year in compliance with the provision of Section 124, Volume I of the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

Likewise, we recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the GSO, Head of Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant and all of them promised to adhere with the guidelines and procedures set forth under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 for the conduct of physical count of properties; recognition of PPE items found at station; disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items; and for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances, in order to reflect more reliable and accurate balances of PPE accounts, hence to convey precise information useful in decision making and accountability for these properties.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee was able to conduct physical count of all properties and submitted the corresponding inventory report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the LCE agreed to:

a) request from the Office of the Sangguniang Bayan to establish guidelines as to the mobile/ communication expenses being received by the department heads and to integrate such expense/ allowance into the payroll system;

b) instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer that the internet services per office be paid through checks directly to the supplier; and

c) direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 on the utilization of PCF, that the same shall be sufficient for the recurring petty operating expenses of the agency for one month. The cash advance shall not be used for payment of regular expenses, such as rentals, subscriptions, light and water bills and the like

We recommended and Management agreed to:

a) Formulate or adopt policies and guidelines promulgated by DSWD on the grant of financial assistance under the AICS program to strengthen the controls employed in the selection of eligible beneficiaries to avoid inequitable allocation to the constituents of the Municipality; and

b) Establish a cash advance exclusively for AICS to facilitate the immediate release of the financial assistance to the beneficiaries in the form of cash payment thus, to address promptly their urgent needs and to alleviate their critical situations.
observations.managementAction: Management was able to formulate policies and guidelines on the grant of financial assistance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the LCE agreed to request from the Sangguniang Bayan for a resolution to convert the payment of employees’ salaries, bonuses and other monetary benefits through ATM in order for the Management to strengthen its internal controls on cash and avoid the possible risk of theft, loss or misappropriation.
observations.managementAction: A resolution was passed by the Sangguniang Bayan for the conversion of the payment of employees’ salaries, bonuses and other monetary benefits through ATM.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended and the Management agreed to:

Submit the required Report of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment showing relevant information, such as property number, unit value, balance of PPE per property cards and per count; and

Undertake all other procedures prescribed under the same Circular to come up with the reconciled and correct balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Management conducted an inventory of its PPE accounts.
observations.reason: The Management is still in the process of finalizing the preparation of its RPCPPE and was not able to conduct all of the one-time cleansing procedures in order to arrive at a reliable balance of its PPE accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:

The Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, to prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and the amounts due and collectible based on updated Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card to provide an accurate and reliable basis in setting up the Receivables from RPT and SET; and

The Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to record the RPT/SET Receivables established at the beginning of the year based on the duly certified list of taxpayers with the amount due and collectible for the year and maintain the corresponding subsidiary ledgers to monitor tax collections and delinquencies.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant was able to maintain subsidiary ledgers pertaining to the CY 2024 set up of RPT and SET Receivables only.
observations.reason:
The Accounting and Treasury Offices have yet to reconcile their records to prepare subsidiary ledgers for the years prior to 
CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to draw the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the reported balance of the affected accounts and ensure that the proper accounting and reporting of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002 and Section 111 (2) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 are strictly complied with to ensure the fair presentation of the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has drawn the necessary journal entries to correct the reported balances of the affected accounts.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MDRRM Officer to strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM related PPAs to achieve the goals and objective of the fund, as well as the desired benefits, in compliance with Republic Act No. 10121.
observations.managementAction: The Management was able to optimally utilize its LDRRMF during the year.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended and the Management agreed to refrain from charging administrative expenses under the 20% Development Fund and strictly adhere with the pertinent provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020 and DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-a dated 
February 22, 2017 in the utilization of the said Fund.
observations.managementAction: The Management has refrained from charging administrative expenses under the 20% Development Fund.
The Management was able to substantially implement its programs, projects and activities under the 20% Development Fund.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended and the Management agreed, thru the Local Chief Executive, to undertake the following courses of action:

Discontinue the practice of dropping items of PPE from the books of accounts without undergoing the proper process of disposal;

Strengthen internal controls pertinent to property accountability and clearance from accountability in order to safeguard government properties against loss or wastage through illegal or improper disposition with a view to ensuring efficiency, economy and effectiveness in the operations of government; and

Consult with the PFDA on any construction to be made on the port site as stated in their Memorandum of Agreement.
observations.managementAction: The Management has refrained from dropping items of PPE from the books of accounts without undergoing the proper disposal procedures.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the accounts, Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds and effect adjustments to reflect the correct account balances and present reliable data pursuant to Section 111(2) of P.D. 1445.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to undertake the following: 

A.	Evaluate and determine the nature and status of the transactions recorded under the Other Payables - Trust Fund in order to come up with an appropriate action or disposition;

Strictly monitor the PPELCs to prevent over-depreciation and occurrences of negative/abnormal balances.

The Municipal Accounting Office has strictly monitored and reviewed the PPELCs, ensuring accurate depreciation and preventing the occurrence of negative or abnormal balances. Regular reconciliation between the PPELCs and the general ledger was conducted, and discrepancies were promptly addressed. As a result, the PPELCs for CY 2024 reflect accurate and normal balances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to evaluate the following: a. Designating additional DOs to eliminate the granting of multiple CAs to a DO; and b. Increasing the amount of bond as a result of the increase in the accountability of the DO. Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Deny request for additional CA if the DO has an existing unliquidated CA; b. Install, implement and monitor a sound system of internal control in granting and subsequent liquidation of CAs; and c. Henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002, and Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of CAs.
observations.managementAction: No action was noted on the part of Management to address the audit recommendation. The OIC – Municipal Treasurer ensured that no cash advance shall exceed the maximum accountability. Hence, there was no necessity to increase the accountability of the DOs. Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Deny request for additional CA if the DO has an existing unliquidated CA; The OIC – Municipal Treasurer ensured that no cash advance shall exceed the maximum accountability. Hence, there was no necessity to increase the accountability of the DOs. Furthermore, the OIC – Municipal Treasurer ensured that no cash advance shall exceed the maximum accountability. An automated teller machine (ATM) payroll system was implemented for the payment of employees’ salaries and wages to reduce reliance on cash advances. As an additional internal control measure, DOs were assigned based on the nature of the cash advance (e.g., Payroll Fund, Operating Expenses, etc.). c. Henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002 and Treasury Circular No. 02-2009. The provisions of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002, and Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 were strictly observed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Officer to: a. Fill-up the vacant plantilla positions, giving priority to qualified casual employees and JO workers; and b. Coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Human Resource Management Officer to thoroughly assess the organizational structure and staffing pattern of the Municipality, taking into consideration its service requirements and financial capability, subject to the minimum standards and guidelines prescribed by the Civil Service Commission.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality has published the position for Municipal Registrar on the Civil Service website. Furthermore, Management disclosed that from 2020 to 2024, a total of 54 Job Order personnel were appointed as Casual Employees, while 41 were appointed as Permanent Employees. Since 2021, the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) has successfully led the correction and streamlining of plantilla positions, resulting in abolition of 18 positions. Additionally, two plantilla positions were filled on December 16, 2022. The Municipality has completed its assessment of the organizational structure through regular communication with department heads, ensuring an appropriate balance between Job Order personnel and Permanent Employees. The HRMO has coordinated with the Local Chief Executive (LCE) and the Sangguniang Bayan to recommend the creation of a resolution for the abolition of unnecessary vacant plantilla positions, which has been approved.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairperson of the BAC and the OIC – Municipal General Services Officer to: a. Submit copies of Contracts, POs and its supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five working days after its perfection/issuance in compliance with Sections 3.1.1 and 3.2.2 of COA Circular No. 2009-001; b. Furnish the Office of the Auditor with a copy of the delivery documents within 24 hours after acceptance of deliveries of goods and services as required under Section 6.9 of COA Circular No. 2009-002 to facilitate the timely inspection of deliveries; and c. Ensure that POs should contain the brand names together with the specifications of the items pursuant to Item B of COA Circular No. 96-010.
observations.managementAction: The OIC – Municipal General Services Officer explained that the delay was caused by the demise of the Municipal General Services Officer. During the time of delay, the Municipality still awaits the appointment of officer-in-charge who will lead the overall affairs of the MGSO. The OIC – Municipal General Services Officer and the BAC Secretariat were directed by the Municipal Mayor through Memorandum Nos. 053 and 053-A Series of 2024 to submit copies of Contracts, delivery documents, POs and its supporting documents within the prescribed period set by the Commission. However, delays were still observed in the submission of Contracts, Purchase Orders (POs), and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor. Management did not provide any explanation regarding the non-implementation of the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant, together with the project manager and the systems focal person, to communicate the noted deficiencies to the developer and to immediately correct the same at no additional cost, or otherwise hold responsible the officials who effected the procurement of the new AIS.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has formally requested the Project Manager of the systems provider, through written correspondence, to promptly rectify the identified deficiencies in the New Accounting System at no additional cost.

observations.recommendation: Furthermore, we advised the Municipal Mayor to direct the Inventory Committee to: a. Prepare an updated Report on RPCPPE based on the actual physical count conducted; b. Implement any other necessary procedures outlined in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.
observations.managementAction: Pursuant to Executive Order No. 35-S’2024 dated May 31, 2024 issued by the Local Chief Executive; the designated members of the Inventory Committee conducted a physical count of all PPE owned by the Municipality. On March 3, 2025, the Inventory Committee, headed by the OIC – Municipal General Services Officer, submitted the RPCPPE based on the results of the physical inventory conducted. However, the property numbers contained in the PPELCs maintained by the MAO did not match the physical count of the PPE. Consequently, they were not reconciled.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO) to: a. Immediately submit the following to the Office of the Auditor: i. A revised LDRRMFIP which includes the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years; and ii. Sangguniang Bayan Resolution or a Presidential Declaration on the release of the 30 percent QRF on the release of the 30 percent QRF amounting to P717,935.00 since there was no declaration of a state of calamity by the Sangguniang Bayan or a Presidential Declaration which is required for the release and use of the 30 percent QRF. b. Strictly adhere to Section 4.3 of NDRRMC – DBM – DILG JMC No. 2013-1 and ensure that the 30 percent QRF should only be used as a stand-by fund for relief and recovery projects and activities. We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that disbursements are duly supported with complete documentation pursuant to fundamental principles under Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: On February 29, 2024 the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (MDRRMO) submitted the revised LDRRMFIP, which includes the PPAs funded by the unexpended balance. The MDRRMO stated that the majority of the procurement activities undertaken were based on immediate preparedness measures anticipated by the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (LDRRMC). These measures addressed the progression from the onset of extreme heat, through the typhoon season, and culminating in the declaration of a State of Calamity for the entire Municipality of Santa Cruz under Sangguniang Bayan Ordinance No. 366‑2024. A copy of pages 132-133 of the LDRRMF Plan for CYs 2023–2025 was presented to the Audit Team showing the proposed procurement of an ambulance/response vehicle under the Plan for CY 2024. Regarding the revised AIP, the MDRRMO informed the Audit Team that assistance will be sought from the Municipal Accountant to ensure the inclusion of the implementation schedule for CY 2025 and that all revisions will be properly endorsed and approved by the appropriate authorities. The Municipal Accountant commits to ensure that all transactions charged against the unexpended LDRRMF balances are properly documented with complete supporting records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to verify the unremitted withholding taxes and immediately remit or effect the necessary adjustment, if warranted. Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office for strictly monitoring monthly preparation of withholding tax and due for remittance to ensure that the balance of Due to BIR account (2-02-01-010) is nil after remittance; and b. Apply the necessary corrective actions on the erroneous taxes withheld on payments for catering services and henceforth, ensure that appropriate withholding taxes, pursuant to Item B(3) of BIR RR No. 23-2012, are withheld from suppliers to avoid imposition of interest and/or surcharges.
observations.managementAction: The MTO coordinated with and provided the MAO a copy of the Summary of Remittances on Withholding Tax for the CY 2023 to verify any unremitted withholding taxes. The MAO applied 2 percent withholding tax on catering services and ensured that appropriate withholding taxes are withheld from suppliers pursuant to Item B(3) of BIR RR No. 23-2012. The MAO, henceforth, committed to strictly monitoring the withholding and subsequent remittances to avoid accumulation of unremitted balances and over-remittance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Human Resource Management Officer to: a. Provide a written explanation on the occurrence of over and/or under remittance of withheld contributions for PhilHealth, and employees’ GSIS and Pag-IBIG premiums and loan amortizations; b. Analyze the SL accounts of the Due to Pag-IBIG (2-02-01-030) account, which exhibit abnormal debit balances of P172,502.04, after the posting of the remittances for the month of December 2023. Draw the corresponding adjusting JEV, if warranted, and furnish the Audit Team for verification; and c. Verify the unremitted PhilHealth contributions and GSIS premiums and/or loan amortizations for CY 2023, and immediately remit or effect the necessary adjustment/s, if warranted. Henceforth, strictly monitor the PhilHealth contributions, Pag-IBIG and GSIS withholding and subsequent remittances of the premium and/or loan amortizations to avoid accumulation of unremitted balances and over-remittance.
observations.managementAction: The MAO and the HRMO provided clarifications regarding the remittances. They stated that remittances undergo thorough checking and reconciliation with the systems of the respective agencies, and remittances not captured in the systems were addressed accordingly. PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG premiums for newly hired and promoted personnel were processed upon approval of their membership applications. The MAO and HRMO will continue to monitor and reconcile the accounts to avoid accumulation of unremitted balances. The MAO and HRMO have applied corrective measures and adjusted remittances where necessary.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor (a) together with the Municipal Accountant to complete and submit to the Audit Team the noted lacking documents in compliance with Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 and Item 13.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (b) to register before the Registry of Deeds the acquired parcel of land in order to secure a TCT/CLT in the name of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction:
Implemented (a) Implemented: The Municipal Assessor submitted the noted lacking documents to the Registry of Deeds, but the LGU failed to secure a photocopy for submission to the Audit Team. Nonetheless, they have already secured the title for the lot.

(b) Implemented: The Municipal Assessor has registered the parcel of land with the Land Registration Authority (LRA) and secured the corresponding land title. A photocopy of the said title was submitted to the Audit Team on August 21, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a. Municipal Agriculturist and the Municipal Accountant to provide immediately the above-noted lacking documents required under COA Circular No. 2007-001 and Item 2.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and written justification on the failure to undertake the required procedures; and b. Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, to ensure that disbursements of funds are supported with complete and accurate documentation.
observations.managementAction:
Implemented The Municipal Agriculturist and the Municipal Accountant submitted a written justification and some of the lacking documents in June 2024. Moreover, people’s organizations have already liquidated the fund or grant of livelihood assistance awarded to them with complete and appropriate documentation.

Implemented The Municipal Accountant, as internal auditor, ensured that disbursements of funds are supported with complete and accurate documentation afterwards.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a. BAC to submit the above-lacking documents relative to the procurement and provide a written explanation on the lapses noted; and b. Municipal Accountant to (i) submit the lacking documents noted in the payments; (ii) secure warranty security on payments of goods procured in accordance with Section 62.1 of the IRR of R.A. No. 9184; and (iii) as the internal auditor, ensure that disbursements of funds are supported with complete and accurate documentation.
observations.managementAction:
Implemented In CY 2024, the BAC had already corrected the lapses noted in the procurement of goods.

(i) Implemented: The lacking documents noted in the payments were submitted.

(ii) Implemented: Warranty security equivalent to one percent was already deducted to the amount due to the supplier.

(iii) Implemented: As the internal auditor, the Municipal Accountant ensured that disbursements of funds were supported with complete and accurate documentation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to (a) review thoroughly the Approved Budget for the Contract, Program of Works and Detailed Engineering Estimates to ensure that the basis and ceiling of bid prices are acceptable/reasonable and advantageous to the government; and (b) submit written explanation or justification on the above reasons/causes of variances noted by the COA TAG, otherwise, deduct the excess amounts from the succeeding claims of the contractor.
observations.managementAction:
Implemented (a) Implemented: The Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) attached to the contracts was reviewed thoroughly and in accordance with the DPWH Guidelines in the preparation of ABC.

(b) Implemented: The Municipal Engineer submitted a written justification dated February 12, 2024 and the same was forwarded to the COA Region IV-B TAG for evaluation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a. Members of the Municipal Development Council to ensure that all the development projects to be funded by the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation ready pursuant to Item 3.2.3 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 to ensure the optimal utilization of the fund; b. Municipal Engineer, being the end-user, to provide in the PPMPs the time schedule of implementation of all projects charged to 20% DF for integration in the APP; and c. BAC to (i) indicate the time schedule for each identified procurement activity for all projects in the APP to efficiently manage the conduct of the procurement transactions; (ii) together with the Municipal Engineer, fast track the implementation of the unimplemented developmental projects both under the current and continuing appropriations; and (iii) submit the lacking documents mentioned above and written explanations on the observations noted in the review of contracts; d. Municipal Accountant to (i) submit the lacking documents noted in the payments; and (ii) as the internal auditor, ensure that disbursements of funds are supported with complete and accurate documentation.
observations.managementAction: Implemented In CY 2024, management convened a meeting to address the noted lapses. The Municipal Engineer promised compliance and the Municipal Mayor expressed willingness to approve the plans and designs after detailed engineering activities. The Municipal Engineer committed to implement the two projects by the 2nd and 3rd quarters of CY 2025. The BAC and Municipal Accountant addressed the procurement/documentation lapses; the noted procedural and documentary lapses in the procurement of infrastructure projects were already addressed.
observations.reason: As at December 31, 2024, there were still PPAs funded by the current and continuing appropriations of 20% DF that were not implemented because the projects were not procurement-and-implementation ready. Absence of time schedules in PPMP/APP and pending detailed engineering studies caused delays; manpower and procedural constraints affected timely implementation. Some PPAs remained unimplemented and time schedules were not indicated in submitted PPMPs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor to submit to the Audit Team the noted lacking documents in compliance with Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 and Item 13.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.managementAction: Implemented Although the enumerated documents were not submitted to the Audit Team due to the failure of the Municipal Assessor to secure photocopies of the documents submitted to the LRA, the title of the land was already secured and a photocopy of which was submitted to the Audit Team on August 21, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal and Engineer/BAC Chairperson to provide the noted lacking documents and explain the declaration of LCRBs despite the deficiencies in their submitted bid documents.
observations.managementAction: Implemented In CY 2024, the noted procedural and documentary lapses in the procurement of infrastructure projects were already addressed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Acting Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal SWDO/GAD Focal Person to explain/justify the delayed payout of the cash assistance and the differences noted between the payroll and date of receipt in confirmation replies and to provide the (i) date of receipt of the cash assistance; and (ii) identification of the beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: Implemented For similar transactions in CY 2024, the Acting Municipal Treasurer required the recipients/payees to indicate their signatures and the date of receipt in the payroll.

observations.recommendation: The Chairperson on the Inventory Committee, the Municipal Accountant, and other concerned officials conduct reconciliation of PPE records maintained by the Accounting Unit and Property Unit as of December 31, 2023 to determine accountability for lost, damaged, and stolen PPE items and in preparation for the conduct of physical count of PPE and rendering the RPCPPE for CY 2024.

The Mayor ensure that:
 
The GSO or the authorized property custodian prepare and submit to the Audit Team the Report on Local Road Networks and maintain the local road inventory, road map and property cards for all roads and their components; 
 
The Inventory Committee conduct physical count of LRN and render the required Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network; and 

The Municipal Accountant comply with the required template in the preparation of Local Road Network Ledger Cards.

The Municipal Treasurer submit to the Audit Team the lacking SSMI for CY 2023, including the stock cards and the RPCI as of December 31, 2023, including a written explanation on the non-submission of such, for further evaluation. 





The Municipal Accountant submit to the Audit Team the related ledgers and supplies ledger cards for all inventory and supplies and materials expenses accounts, including a written explanation on the non-submission of such, for further evaluation.
The Municipal Accountant make a JEV to adjust the unspent balance of the LDRRMF to exclude the unspent capital outlay of ₱57,600.00.

The Municipal Mayor ensure the installation of sufficient internal controls over the safeguarding of cash, accountable forms, and other assets in the Office of the Municipal Treasurer through:

Purchase and installation of safety vaults and steel cabinets with vaults where undeposited collections can be safekept; 

Purchase and installation of a locked and fireproof steel cabinet or safety vaults where all unissued accountable forms can be safekept; and

Installation of protective mechanisms or barriers on all doors and windows of the Office to prevent unauthorized access.

The Municipal Mayor submit a written explanation on the utilization of ₱8,469,690.38 without specific sanggunian approval, including submission of additional supporting documents for further evaluation thereof.

The Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Budget Officer, establish controls to ensure that the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF be determined before year end so that such balance can be transferred to the Trust Fund through the issuance of check within the same calendar year. 

The Municipal Accountant submit to the Audit Team a copy of the disbursement voucher of the transfer of unspent balance to the trust fund for further evaluation.

The Municipal Mayor and HRMO:

Initiate steps in filling-in the vacant positions of the LDRRMO in accordance with civil service rules to assure implementation of actions and measures related to disaster risk reduction and management; 

Submit an explanation on the noted creation and appointment of the Municipal Government Department Head as head of the LDRRMO contrary to Item 5.3 of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1, including proof that the Municipality is financially capable and has not exceeded the PS limitation; and

Submit a written representation with the Sangguniang Bayan to revise the ordinance creating the LDRRMO, taking into consideration revising the salary grades of the LDRRMO personnel in compliance with Item 5.3 of the NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014.

The Municipal Mayor and other concerned officials:

Revise the Executive Order (EO) on the establishment of the GFPS taking into consideration compliance with Item 4.1(A).2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended, with the end in view of making the GFPS well-represented among all sectors in their jurisdiction; and 

Submit to the Audit Team a copy of: (a) latest GAD Database; (b) GAD Code; and (c) an EO establishing the M&E System, if any, including a written explanation for the non-submission of such, for further evaluation.

The Municipal Mayor:

Confer with the PCW, the DILG, or other government authorities on the availability of capability trainings as required in Item 4.1(A)(3) of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended;

Appropriate sufficient funds for these capability-building programs; and

Require all elected officials and department heads to attend all of the required capability-building programs with the end in view of applying such capabilities towards the effective discharge and implementation of the Magna Carta of Women.

The BAC issue written notices within a reasonable period to the BAC Secretariat before each stage for the procurement of goods, infrastructure projects, or consultancy services to ensure that invitation letters were received by the required observers at least five calendar days prior to the earliest scheduled procurement activity.

The BAC issue periodic written notices within a reasonable period before each of the deadlines of submission to the BAC Secretariat to ensure compliance by the latter with GPPB Circular No. 02-2020 dated May 20, 2020.

The Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned officials/employees to deduct retention money or require a special bank guarantee in the procurement of goods, otherwise any expenses that may be incurred due to the defects within the warranty period will be charged to the personal account of the concerned approving officials.

The Municipal Mayor and/or other concerned officials make representation with the service provider for the conduct of training to orient the Municipal Budget Officer and other employees in the Budget office on the operation of the E-budget system so that it will be fully utilized and be able to provide efficient services to its clientele and other stakeholders.  Otherwise, the engagement with the service provider be temporarily discontinued until such time that the MBO and other employees are capable and able to use the system.

Municipal Treasurer to inquire with the depository bank the details of the debit memo in January 2023 amounting to ₱33,431.97, and subsequently furnish the Municipal Accountant with the documents to record the appropriate adjustments in the books of accounts; and

Municipal Accountant agreed to record all valid and duly supported reconciling items in the books of accounts.

To use a portion of the GAD budget for payment of professional fees, honoraria and other services for gender experts or gender specialists engaged to strengthen GFPS capability by updating and enhancing their skills through continuing gender education customized according to their functions to fully equip the GFPS in the formulation of their annual GPBs within the context of their mandates and other GAD processes to ensure gender mainstreaming in their policies, programs and projects.

We recommended that Management:
1. Require the Property and Accounting Units to conduct collaborative procedures in reconciling the difference noted of the balances of the PPE accounts as presented in the Financial Statements and balances in the RPCPPE to support the accuracy and fair presentation of the PPE account balances.
2. Require the General Services Officer to maintain an updated file of persons in actual possession of the property by renewing their Property Acknowledgment Receipts (PAR) to facilitate immediate monitoring of Accountable persons and their liabilities in case of loss, damage, or deterioration due to negligence in the keeping and use of property. Thus, accountability/responsibility over the missing PPE could be established, and accountability on the said PPE could be properly derecognized in the books of accounts.
3. Require the GSO and the Municipal Accountant to apply the necessary procedures in the conduct of physical count of PPE, recognition of PPE items found at station, and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items for the one-time cleansing of PPE Account balances as provided in COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management through the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) submit all the copies of the perfected contracts together with the supporting documents to the Auditor within five (5) working days from the execution of contract as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2009-001 to facilitate timely review and evaluation of compliance with the requirements of applicable laws, rules and regulations and completeness of documentary requirements thus, enable the auditor to inform the management of any deficiencies that may be observed for immediate and appropriate action.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned Department Heads to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit all the required reports as prescribed in the aforementioned COA Circular and strictly observe the same and install sound internal control. Also, the Municipal Accountant submit all the necessary reports together with the complete supporting documents for the immediate communication of observations relating to financial/operational deficiencies for appropriate action.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Implementing Offices or focal persons to retrieve and turn-over available records to facilitate formulation of appropriate strategies to recover the amount of fund transfers to NGOs/POs. Also, we recommend that Management through the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer send notices to enforce submission of liquidation report and adopt other measures to collect such receivables to allow the Municipal Government of additional funds to finance more developmental projects.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.
observations.reason: Reiterated in observation no. 7.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management observe strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Section 89 of PD 1445 and liquidate fully all cash advances at the end of the year or as soon as the purpose for which it was granted has been served and no additional cash advance shall be allowed to any official or employee unless the previous cash advance given to him is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made to avoid misappropriation and possible loss of government funds. Non-observance of the above-cited rules on the liquidation of cash advances, we recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to withhold the salaries of the erring AO or employees until their obligation is fully refunded.
observations.managementAction: Some unliquidated cash advances were collected during the year.
observations.reason: Reiterated in observation no. 9.

b. Consider all the requisites in the release and utilization of funds to place the necessary control and promote transparency and accountability in the implementation of project before granting any fund transfers to NGOs/POs.
