We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to cause the annual determination of loan amortization due in the next 12 months from the Statement of Financial Position date and reclassify it under the current portion of the Financial Liabilities, pursuant to Par. 80 of PPSAS No. 1.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant had already reclassified the amount to current. However, during the CY 2023 audit, the total Loans Payable account was again classified as non-current, not identifying the current portion of the account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MHO, henceforth, ensure that drugs and medicines procured are included in the PNF 10th Edition (2022) or the latest edition applicable thereof, pursuant to DOH AO No. 2016-0034 dated August 9, 2016, otherwise, the appropriate approval of exemption request should support the procurement. We also recommended that the BAC, henceforth, require certification from the requisitioning officer or by the duly authorized officer that the product being requisitioned and procured fall within and conform with the PNF Essential Medicines List to support the PR, in accordance with the aforementioned DOH AO.
observations.managementAction: The MHO acknowledged that they procured some medicines exceeding the DPRI due to a lapse in monitoring the prices from the latest DPRI issued by the DOH. Management assured that henceforth, the latest DPRI will be used as the ceiling price for procuring drugs and medicines.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management install internal control mechanisms by using the RIS to request and issue supplies and material carried in stock and to consolidate the same in the SSMI, which shall be submitted to the Municipal Accountant for recording in the appropriate expense accounts for the supplies consumed. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop the practice of recording purchases of supplies and materials directly to expense accounts and henceforth, record purchases and utilization of supplies and materials in accordance with Section 114 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I and Section IV of COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014, to fairly present the expense and inventory accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and the MHO implemented the use of RIS and consolidated the SSMI and attached supporting documents. The Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of posting purchases directly to expense accounts and to record them in accordance with Section 114 of NGAS and COA Circular No. 2014-002.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: 12. Ensure that the Bids and Awards Committee and the Municipal Engineer prioritize the implementation of projects funded by grants from various national government agencies, especially those with ongoing and for implementation status, by the specific purposes as stated in the Memorandum of Agreement; and 13. Direct the Office of the Municipal Accountant to reconcile outstanding balances in the Due from NGAs account to the corresponding source agencies and obtain certification of those balances which need to be liquidated to be able to act appropriately. We also recommended that Management re-evaluate and verify the status of all projects and activities related to the funds due to NGAs. Any unused balances from completed projects should be returned. Alternatively, request authority from the source agency to use the balances for other projects that require additional funding. For unimplemented projects, we recommended and Management also agreed to assess their viability and necessity. If they are deemed viable and necessary, we suggested and Management agreed to promptly pursue their implementation.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer explained that manpower and heavy equipment in the Municipal Government are limited. However, Management intends to address these issues in 2024. The Municipal Accountant explained that some of these accounts were already reconciled and the updated schedule of Due to NGAs as of July 14, 2023 was already submitted to COA last June 1, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management, in coordination with the MSWD, to establish the following remedial measures to regulate the grant of financial assistance: 18. Amend the policies and guidelines on the grant of financial assistance in conformity with DSWD MC No. 15, s. 2022, as amended; and 19. Always refer cases to the MSWDO for eligibility evaluation duly supported by a case study report.
observations.managementAction: Implemented. Management amended the policies and guidelines and now always refers cases to the MSWDO for eligibility evaluation duly supported by a case study report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management take proactive measures in being informed and updated of relevant issuances by authorities that may affect their local governance performance scorecards, such as the DILG’s Seal of Good Local Governance, and comply with provisions therein, like the submission of PSCP.
observations.managementAction: Management had already took proactive measures to be informed and updated of relevant issuances by authorities that may affect their local governance performance scorecards, such as the DILG’s Seal of Good Local Governance, and comply with provisions therein, like the submission of PSCP.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MDRRMO, MENRO, MEO hasten the implementation of LDDRM PPAs pursuant to Items 4.0 and 5.0 of NDDRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013. We further recommended that the BAC expedite the procurement of the LDRRM PPAs. Otherwise, the BAC will require the MDRRMO to revise the cost estimates of the various equipment, tools and medical supplies based on the current market prices.
observations.managementAction: Management explained that the cost estimate for each item is too low that no supplier quoted during the bidding. Thus, some identified project/s were realigned to other DRRM item. The LDRRM Funds for prior year had been re-programmed to other items which are within the budget.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management conduct an inventory of its insurable properties, appropriate the necessary premiums in its annual budget. We also recommend that Management insure the same with the Property Insurance Fund under the Government Service Insurance System.
observations.managementAction: Management had already conducted an inventory of its insurable properties. Majority of the properties have already been insured, and the insurances of all municipal vehicles were also updated. However, the documents of the old buildings still cannot be located.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management establish the GAD database storage of necessary information in aid of effective gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-1 to ensure that the PPAs are aligned with GAD-related undertakings so that the intended beneficiaries are assured of the services, protection and other assistance that may be provided.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the Community-Based Monitoring System data of the Municipality of Sta. Catalina had already been available. Thus, data needed in developing the GAD Database is no longer a problem.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant and the GSO to secure the Transfer Certificate of Titles in the
name of the Municipality or other necessary documents as proof of
ownership from the Office of the Municipal Assessor and Municipal
Planning and Development Officer to facilitate the recording of the land
owned by the Municipality in the books of the LGU. Also, we recommended
management to ensure that the proof of ownership is established before the
construction/implementation of projects that are permanent in nature to
avoid the risk of wastage of government funds.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant and
the Municipal Treasurer to conduct regular monitoring and analysis of
accounts receivable and send notices to enforce collection of receivables
which are due and demandable to recover government funds and ensure
that loss or wastage of the same be prevented as provided in Section 2 of
PD 1445. Also, we recommended that management require the Municipal
Accountant to determine the dormant accounts/ doubtful receivable
accounts and set-up allowance for impairment.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the General Services
Officer to dispose of the unserviceable properties as prescribed in Section
356, Title VI, Book II of Local Government Code and Section 79 of PD
1445. Also, follow strictly the rules and regulations in the disposal of
unserviceable properties specified in COA Circular 92-386 to avoid
further deterioration of the properties and to avail of additional income
that could be generated from the sale of the same. Also, for disposition of
non-existing/missing PPE items, consider the one-time cleansing of PPE
Account balances as provided in COA Circular 2020-006 dated January
31, 2020.

We recommended that Management:
1. Require the Property Unit and the Accounting Unit to conduct
collaborative procedures in reconciling the difference noted in the
balances of the PPE account as presented in the Financial Statements
with the balances in the RPCPPE to support the accuracy and fair
presentation of the PPE account balances.
2. Require the General Services Officer (GSO) to maintain an updated
file of persons in actual possession of the property to facilitate
immediate monitoring of Accountable persons and their liabilities in
case of loss, damage, or deterioration occasioned by negligence in the
keeping and use of property.
3. Require the GSO and the Municipal Accountant to apply the necessary
procedures in the conduct of physical count of PPE, recognition of
PPE items found at station, and disposition for non-existing/missing
PPE items for the one-time cleansing of PPE Account balances as
provided in COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended that Management, require the Municipal Accountant to
reconcile the differences noted in the Cash in Bank balances from among
the accounts maintained by the agency with the balances issued by the
banks through bank confirmation to have an accurate Cash in Bank
balance and fair presentation of the same in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to
strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 92-382. Remit the
collections daily and deposit intact with the authorized depository bank.
Deposit on the next working day the balance of collections not deposited
during the day due to cut-off. Also, deposit immediately all the long over-
due undeposited collections to avoid possible misuse and exposure of the
funds to risk of loss or theft. Furthermore, we recommended the Municipal
Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile their books to reflect
the correct balance of Cash Local Treasury Account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to prepare the necessary
documents to facilitate the immediate remittance of the withheld
contributions to the respective government agencies to avoid the
incurrence of penalties and other implications that may arise affecting the
claims of the concerned employees because of delayed remittances of the
said contributions.

To ensure that financial assistance granted is valid, regular and reasonable,
we recommended that Management:
1. Formulate policies and guidelines or adopt the policies and
guidelines promulgated by DSWD on the grant of financial
assistance under the AICS program to strengthen the
controls employed in the selection of eligible beneficiaries;
and
2. Ensure that the MSWDO strictly assess and evaluate
applicants requesting financial assistance out of the AICS
program including the identification of nearest kin who
serves as the claimant;

We recommended that Management through the Bids and Awards
Committee (BAC) submit all the copies of the perfected contracts together
with the supporting documents to the Auditor within five (5) working days
from the execution of contract as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2009-001
to facilitate timely review and evaluation of compliance with the
requirements of applicable laws, rules and regulations and completeness of
documentary requirements thus, enable the auditor to inform the
management of any deficiencies that may be observed for immediate and
appropriate action.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned Department
Heads to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit
all the required reports as prescribed in the aforementioned COA Circular
and strictly observe the same and install sound internal control. Also,
require the Municipal Accountant to submit all the necessary reports
together with the complete supporting documents in order not to delay the
post-audit of transactions and preparation of other reports thus, any
discrepancies could be communicated to management for immediate and
appropriate action thereof.

We recommended that Management, require the Municipal Accountant and
GSO to follow the inventory accounting system/process including the forms
to be used and the reports to be prepared for the inventory to present the
actual status of Inventory of Supplies held for Consumption and require
the immediate issuance of the supplies to the suppose end-user so that the
latter would avail of the advantages from using the said supplies. Also,
avoid overstocking of perishable and time-sensitive goods to prevent
possible wastage of funds from maintaining expired or obsolete products.

We recommended that management instruct the Municipal Accountant to
immediately enforce the settlement of the audit suspensions and
disallowances as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated
September 15, 2009. A suspension shall be settled within ninety (90)
calendar days from receipt of the NS to avoid maturing of the same into
disallowances and a disallowance shall be settled within six (6) months
from receipt of the ND by the persons liable so as not deprived the
Municipal Government for the funds which could be used to fund other
priority projects for the benefit of the constituents.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer to impose that progress payments for the procurement of infrastructure projects are subject to retention money of ten percent (10%) or substitution of the retention money for each progress billing with irrevocable standby letters of credit from a commercial bank, bank guarantees or surety bonds callable on demand, of amounts equivalent to the retention money substituted for and acceptable to government, provided that the project is on schedule and is satisfactorily undertaken to cover uncorrected discovered defects and third party liabilities.

Implemented
deducted 10% Retention in the payment of infrastructure projects
Verification of DV for the payment of infrastructure projects showed that 10% Retention was deducted in the payment of infrastructure projects

observations.recommendation: We recommended Management to ensure that the 20% Development Fund be optimally utilized to help achieve the desirable socio-economic developments and environmental outcomes of the LGU and undertake critical, urgent, and appropriate Covid-19 response aid and measure to curtail and eliminate the threat of COVID-19 through strict adherence with the updated guidelines expressly prescribed under the DILG-DBM JMC Nos. 2017-01 dated February 22, 2017 and 01 dated March 27, 2020. Thus, the fund shall not be used for any purpose beyond those provided by the laws, rules and regulations in order to attain the purpose/s for which the fund was created.

We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Accountant:

Pro-rate the RATA of the Members of the Sanggunian Bayan based on the actual work/attendance of the official. Consider the absences incurred by the Members of the Sanggunian Bayan as deduction in their monthly claims and strictly adhere to the above cited provisions regarding the payment of Representation and Transportation Allowance (RATA) of the Members of the Sanggunian Bayan so as to avoid the issuance of notice of disallowance in audit. Payments should be supported with attendance sheet/minutes of the regular session duly signed by the Secretary to the Sanggunian Bayan.

Require the submission of the Daily Time Records (DTRs) of the Officials/Department Heads who are entitled to receive RATA to support their claims and strictly comply with the provisions of DBM LBC No. 103 dated May 15, 2013 which prescribes rationalized and updated rules and regulations on the grant of Representation and Transportation Allowances (RATA).
observations.managementAction: Overpayment of RATA was discussed with concerned officials and employees together with the schedule of payments.
observations.reason: Verification showed that the Municipal Accountant deducted/pro-rated the absences incurred by the employees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Office of the Municipal Accountant consider the circumstances cited on the above provisions of DBM and DOH Joint Circular wherein PHWs are not entitled to Subsistence Allowance and Laundry Allowance and pro-rate the computation of such allowances based on the number of days of actual services rendered within the premises of RHU in a given month to avoid overpayment.

observations.reason: Verification showed that the schedule for their field work was submitted and some employees were deducted from theirallowance for the days that they did not report for work.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure that no government funds shall be used for fuel of privately owned vehicles. The plate number of the government vehicle to be used shall be indicated in the driver’s trip ticket. Payment of fuel consumption of the municipality shall be properly controlled and monitored to prevent consumption of private vehicles being charged against the credit line of the municipality with the gasoline station, otherwise improper application of government funds for private purposes shall be subject to disallowance in audit if warranted.
observations.managementAction: Management implemented controls in the issuance of driver’s trip tickets.
observations.reason: Verification showed that driver’s trip tickets were issued only to government vehicles.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management adhere strictly with the provisions of the Local Government Code on the use of savings as a fund source for a supplemental budget under Section 321 of R.A. No. 7160 and the use of savings for augmentation under Section 336 of R.A. No. 7160. Also, management must refrain from realigning savings from one expense class and utilizing the same for another expense class.

observations.reason: Verification showed that management observed the guidelines issued especially the realignment of funds to address the COVID-19 issues.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management, through the Municipal Accountant, strictly observe the above cited provision by promptly submitting all the necessary reports together with their complete supporting documents. Billboard in the preparation of Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) and
Program of Work (POW) as one of the pay items and require the winning bidders
to display the tarpaulin signboards on the project sites to inform the public of the
essential features/elements of the PPAs.

We recommended that Management, through the Bids and Awards
Committee require the submission of Construction Safety and Health Program
approved by DOLE for every infrastructure project to be implemented before
signing of the contract. Cost of item of work included in the preparation of
Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) and included as one of the pay items in
the Program of Work which does not adhere with existing rules and regulations
shall be subject to disallowance in audit.

We recommended that management ensure that the 20% Development
Fund be optimally utilized to help achieve the desirable socio-economic
developments and environmental outcomes of the LGU through strict adherence
with updated guidelines. Based on the foregoing, the fund should not be used for
any purpose beyond those expressly prescribed under the DILG-DBM Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 2017-01 dated February 22, 2017. It is worth
mentioning that the utilization of the 20% DF, whether willfully or through
negligence, for any purpose other than those expressly prescribed by law or public
policy shall be subjected to the sanctions provided under RA No. 7160 and other
applicable laws.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary
documents to facilitate the immediate remittance of the withheld contributions to
the respective government agencies. Subsequently, the Municipal Treasurer
should remit immediately the withheld contributions upon preparation by the
Municipal Accountant of the necessary documents pursuant to existing
government rules and regulations. Such practice may prevent the incurrence of
probable penalties and dilemma as regards the individual accounts of its
personnel.

We recommended that management immediately enforce the settlement of
the audit suspensions and disallowances as prescribed under COA Circular No.
2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. A suspension shall be settled within ninety
(90) calendar days from receipt of the NS and a disallowance shall be settled
within six (6) months from receipt of the ND by the persons liable.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive designate a new committee to conduct physical inventory of all the Property, Plant and Equipment so as to determine their existence, correct valuation and serviceability. Likewise, the Report of Physical Count of Property, Plant & Equipment (RPCPPE) should be submitted to the auditor concerned for verification not later than January 31 of each year.
observations.managementAction: The municipality conducted partial physical inventory of PPE and was not able to reconcile the balances of PPE in the financial statements with the balances in the RPCPPE.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the provision stated above. Collections for the day should be deposited intact with the authorized depository bank. The balance of collections not deposited during the day due to cut-off should be deposited in the next working day to avoid risks from using the collection for paying obligations and expose the funds to risk from loss or theft.
observations.managementAction: Verification of the Financial Report showed that the Cash Local Treasury Account has a balance of P27,946.21 representing undeposited collections of the Special Education Fund (SEF). The balance of collections not deposited during the day due to cut-off should be deposited on the next working day to avoid risks of using the collection for paying obligations and exposure of the funds to risk of loss or theft.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 2 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Municipal Accountant prepare and submit monthly reconciliation statements in order to establish the accuracy and/or correctness of the Cash in Bank balances as required in COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant submitted the 2019 Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management observe strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Section 89 of PD 1445 and liquidate fully all cash advances at the end of the year or as soon as the purpose for which it was granted has been served and no additional cash advance shall be allowed to any official or employee unless the previous cash advance given to him is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made to avoid abuse and possible loss of government funds. Non-observance of the above-cited rules on the liquidation of cash advances shall constitute a valid cause for the withholding of salaries of erring accountable officers or employees.
observations.managementAction: Review of the financial statements showed that Advances to Officers and Employees account had a balance of P2,099,374.81.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management grant special cash advances only to duly designated and properly bonded Disbursing Officers or Accountable Officers. The Accountable Officer shall prepare liquidation report with complete supporting documents and submit the same to the Accountant within the prescribed period as required in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government granted Cash Advances for special purposes to the designated disbursing officers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management through the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the above cited provision. Submit on time all the necessary reports together with their complete supporting documents in order not to delay the post audit of transactions and preparation of other reports. It is the direct responsibility of the agency head to install, implement, and monitor sound internal control.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant exerted effort to meet the deadline in the submission of accounts. However there were some unavoidable circumstances in the timely submission of the accounts.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 9 of this report

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management submit legal basis to justify the payments of honorarium to a non-government personnel/a private individual sitting as MFARM Chairman as to its legality, regularity and propriety, otherwise such payments shall be refunded.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government discontinued the granting of honorarium to the MFARM Chairman.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer control and monitor the issuance of Obligation Request (OBR) for every transaction. Obligation Request (OBR) should be made available prior to the issuance of Invitation to Bid and should be forwarded to the Head of the Procuring Entity before entering into a contract to prove that there is available appropriation and there is authority issued by the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to incur obligations. Likewise, only one Obligation Request (OBR) must be prepared for each contract.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government prepared 1 Obligation Request for various progress billing only after the issuance of AOM

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: 1. Refrain from making an advance payment for services not yet rendered or for supplies and materials not yet delivered under any contract. 2. Through the Municipal Accountant require the necessary supporting documents before payment shall be made. 3. Through the Municipal Accountant use the appropriate account for advance payment/prepayments and not as an outright expense.
observations.managementAction: No advance payment was made to various hospitals for the Health Services of the LGU.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that in the payment of overtime services to government employees, provisions of CSC and DBM Joint Circular No. 2, s. 2015 dated November 25, 2015 must be strictly observed. The Agency Head shall be held responsible for the proper implementation of the provisions of the above Joint Circular; any unauthorized or excess payments shall be refunded by the employees concerned.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government did not include personnel not allowed to claim overtime pay.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: 1. Through the Municipal Accountant require all the necessary documents before payment to prove the validity and legality of the transaction. 2. Submit legal basis to justify the payment of honorarium to officials and employees of the Municipal Government, otherwise such payment shall be refunded.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government discontinued the payment of honorarium.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare immediately the disbursement vouchers covering such remittances and supporting statements to the Local Government Units (LGUs) to avoid possible misappropriation/misuse of the funds.
observations.managementAction: Verification of the collection of the Disbursement Vouchers revealed that the Mun. Aid and RPT Share of the different barangays for Jan- July 2019 were already transferred.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Municipal Accountant look into the circumstances cited on the above provisions of DBM and DOH Joint Circular and base the computation of the Subsistence Allowance and Laundry Allowance on the number of days of actual work performance rendered in a month.
observations.managementAction: Verification of the monthly claims for Subsistence Allowance & Laundry Allowance of the employees assigned in the RHU showed that fixed amounts were received despite the absences incurred.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the above cited provisions regarding the payment of Representation and Transportation Allowance (RATA) of the Members of the Sanggunian Bayan so as to avoid the issuance of notice of disallowance in audit. Payments should be supported with attendance sheet/minutes of the regular session duly signed by the Secretary to the Sanggunian Bayan.
observations.managementAction: Verification of the payment of RATA of the Members of the Sanggunian Bayan revealed that the leave of absence/s incurred by the Members of the Sanggunian Bayan of the municipality during their regular sessions were not considered as deductions in the payment of Representation and Transportation Allowance (RATA).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Municipal Government consider the benefits they could derive from insuring their properties with the “Property Insurance Fund” under the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), to indemnify the government of any damage to, or loss of, its properties due to fire, earthquake, storm or other calamities.
observations.managementAction: Verification of the Disbursement Vouchers revealed that the Municipal Government insured their government vehicles with the GSIS. Other PPEs were not included.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Municipal Government send collection notices and exert all efforts to collect said receivables from the concerned beneficiaries to ensure that loss or wastage of government funds be prevented as provided in Section 2 of PD 1445. To safeguard all the resources of the government, an efficient and effective collection scheme must be adhered to.
observations.managementAction: Verification of the Report of Status of Fund Transfers to NGOs/POs showed that no settlement was made.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 4 of this report

observations.recommendation: We recommended that GAD Plan and Budget and the corresponding Accomplishment Report be submitted to oversight agencies on due date as required under Sections 4.1.C.5 and 4.1.C.6 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016 to ensure the relevance of the GAD PPAs to the identified gender issues and their causes as well as the correctness and alignment of the entries in the GPB Form.
observations.managementAction: Verification of the GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipal Government revealed that no proof of receipt from the oversight agencies appeared on the report.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 10 of this report

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management immediately transfer the unexpended balance of the QRF and MF-MOOE from the current year appropriation to the Trust Fund books using the “Trust Liability – DRRM” in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-2002 dated September 12, 2012 to avoid possible misappropriation of the fund other than for the support of disaster risk management activities.
observations.managementAction: The unexpended balance of Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) from the current year appropriations amounting to P2,251,010.97 was not transferred to a Special Trust Fund account, Trust Liability-DRRM.
observations.reason: The unexpended balance of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) from the current year appropriations amounting to P2,251,010.97 was not transferred to a Special Trust Fund account, Trust Liability-DRRM.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipality utilize the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (MDRRMF) in line with the purposes as provided in Section 3.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012. The appropriated funds for MDRRM shall be used to support disaster risk management activities and refrain from using the fund for other activities of the agency which can be properly appropriated in the general fund.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government utilized the MDRRMF for disaster risk management activities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management submit all the copies of approved government contracts/purchase orders together with the supporting documents to the auditor within five (5) working days from the execution of the contract for review to avoid doubt on the validity of the contracts and to ensure the submission of all the necessary supporting documents before paying the transactions.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government submitted some of the government contracts for review within the prescribed period.

observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 21 of this report

observations.managementAction: The Municipality conducted Pre-procurement conference on some of the procurements for 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended management that in the preparation of their Approved Budget for the Contract pertaining to infrastructure projects, the cost of implementing a Construction Safety and Health Program to be approved by the Department of Labor and Employment shall be included. Relative thereto, every contractor should be required to comply with the implementation of the same.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality required the submission of the Construction Safety and Health Program in the implementation of infrastructure projects.
observations.reason: The Municipality was able to include in the Program of Work the Construction Safety and Health Program in some of the infrastructure projects implemented in CY 2018

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the municipal government faithfully monitor and evaluate the implementation of its projects so that any cause of suspension or problems will be immediately addressed.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government limit the issuance of suspension work order for the implementation of infrastructure projects
observations.reason: The Municipal Government was able to minimize the issuance of suspension work order for the implementation of some of the infrastructure projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the municipal government strictly enforce the provision of Section 62.1 of the revised IRR of RA 9184 in order to fully protect the interest of the government in case of manufacturing defects.
observations.reason: The Municipal Government required the submission of surety bond in lieu of the 10% retention money

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer’s Office further intensify the collection of realty taxes in order to increase income to carry out plans and objectives of the municipality.
observations.reason: The Municipal Government was not able to fully implement the Revenue Collection Policies in the collection of Real Property Taxes but they are coordinating with the Provincial Government for other remedies to be implemented to intensify the collection of Real Property Taxes

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the municipality conduct annual physical inventory of all supplies or property of the local government unit as of December 31 of each year and submit the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) to the auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year. Likewise, the General Services Office should keep and maintain property cards per category of Property, Plant and Equipment.

We recommended that the municipal government request authority from the Commission on Audit to write-off the receivables in conformity with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 and the payables may be reverted in accordance with Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445 otherwise known as The Government Auditing Code of the Philippines.

We recommended that the Accountable Officers observe strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 on the liquidation of cash advances.  It should be noted that COA Circular No. 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012 provides sanctions that could be instituted to accountable officers to settle their cash advances.
We recommended that the Petty Cash Fund Custodian return the unused petty cash at the end of the year as provided by the above-cited accounting rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipality acquire insurance policies for all its insurable properties with the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) to provide an indemnification scheme for the agency in case of losses arising from fortuitous events.

We recommended that the municipality strictly comply with the disbursement procedure for the Special Education Fund. Proper certification by the District Supervisor on Special Education Fund disbursements should be observed.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that management require the BAC to strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184 and its revised IRR to ensure that procurement is effected in an efficient, economical and effective manner that is most advantageous to the government.

We recommended management that in the preparation of their Approved Budget for the Contract pertaining to infrastructure projects, the cost of implementing a Construction Safety and Health Program to be approved by the Department of Labor and Employment shall be included. Relative thereto, every contractor should be required to comply with the implementation of the same.
We recommended that the municipal government faithfully monitor and evaluate the implementation of its projects so that any cause of suspension or problems will be immediately addressed.

We recommended that the municipal government strictly enforce the provision of Section 62.1 of the revised IRR of RA 9184 in order to fully protect the interest of the government in case of manufacturing defects.
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer’s Office further intensify the collection of realty taxes in order to increase income to carry out plans and objectives of the municipality.

We recommended that the municipal employees and officials responsible for the preparation and implementation/utilization of the budget of the municipality see to it that usage of the 20% Development Fund be in accordance with the set guidelines.

We recommended that management closely monitor and enforce full settlement of the audit disallowance. Henceforth, ensure that rules and regulations are strictly complied with before effecting payments to avoid disallowance in audit.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor see to it that inventory taking be conducted and completed as soon as possible and a Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) be prepared and furnish the Auditor a copy for verification.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed that all departments will work together to address the audit observation.
observations.reason:
Based on evaluation and inquiry, it was noted that the municipality did not conduct physical count of its properties and submit the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE). In addition, property cards were not maintained.

Discussed in Observation No. 1 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the municipal government request authority from the Commission on Audit to write-off the receivables in conformity with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 and the payables may be reverted in accordance with Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445 otherwise known as The Government Auditing Code of the Philippines.
observations.managementAction: Management did not observe the audit recommendation. Nevertheless, they assured that the matter at hand will be given immediate attention in order to clean the books of accounts of the agency and eventually fairly presenting the affected accounts in the financial statements.
observations.reason:
Verification and analysis of the accounts of the municipality revealed that there were unaccounted receivables and payables totaling to P7,045,764.88 due to nonexistence of records.

Reiterated in Observation No. 2 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the local officials assist and provide the Municipal Accountant with the required reports to facilitate the recording back in the books of accounts the 25% of the Public Infrastructures recorded in the registry as of December 31, 2015.  Likewise, the Local Chief Executive shall enjoin the LGU department heads compliance to COA Circular No. 2016-004 to facilitate the implementation of the accounting system and the New Chart of Accounts.
observations.managementAction: The recognition in the books of accounts of Public Infrastructures previously transferred to registries was complied by the municipality. Adjustments were already made by the Municipal Accountant to update the recognition of Public Infrastructures as at the end of the year.
observations.reason: Analysis on the Property, Plant and Equipment account revealed that Public Infrastructures previously transferred to registries were recorded back in the books of accounts.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Accountable Officers observe strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 on the liquidation of cash advances.  It should be noted that COA Circular No. 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012 provides sanctions that could be instituted to accountable officers to settle their cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Some of the outstanding cash advances as of year-end were already liquidated as of the first quarter of the year 2018. Also, some of the persons accountable in the cash advance agreed to give immediate attention for the full settlement of the same.
observations.reason:
In the review of the financial statements, it was noted that the unliquidated cash advance amounted to P2,411,172.55 as of December 31, 2017.

Reiterated in Observation No. 3 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the municipal government carefully review and faithfully adhere to the provisions of the Memorandum of Agreement to ensure the successful implementation of the proposed project and achieve the desired social outcome thereby, contributing to the government’s national goal of reducing poverty through the provision of additional employment to the constituents.
observations.managementAction: The municipal government was able to comply with the audit recommendation.
observations.reason: During the year under audit, it was noted that the cited audit observation had not transpired in the transactions of the municipal government.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the municipal officials and employees strictly observe indication of time in the Itineraries of Travel to facilitate the verification of the validity of per diems claimed.
observations.managementAction: The audit observation was partially addressed by the municipal government in its transactions for the year.
observations.reason: Post-audit of transactions during the year disclosed that the municipal government is generally compliant with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the municipal government adhere strictly to the provisions of RA 9184 and its revised IRR to ensure that procurement is effected in an efficient, economical and effective manner that is most advantageous to the government.
observations.managementAction: Management especially the BAC assured the audit team that their succeeding procurements and projects will be closely monitored in order for the municipality to strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184.
observations.reason:
Audit of transactions of the municipal government for the year revealed that there have been observed circumstances of noncompliance to the revised IRR of RA 9184.

Discussed in Observation Nos. 7-9 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the municipal government strictly enforce the provision of Section 62.1 of the revised IRR of RA 9184 in order to fully protect the interest of the government in case of manufacturing defects.
observations.managementAction: Management did not observe the audit recommendation. Nevertheless, they signified their willingness to comply with it in their succeeding transactions.
observations.reason:
In the post-audit of transactions during the year, it was noted that warranty security was not required from suppliers for the procured goods.

Reiterated in Observation No. 10 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer’s Office further intensify the collection of the delinquent realty taxes in previous years in order to increase income to carry out plans and objectives of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed to further intensify their collection scheme for the RPT and SET especially those considered delinquent.
observations.reason: In the verification and analysis of accounts, it was noted that only the total amount of P1,760,885.56 was collected for RPT and SET. The amount collected represents 29.60% only of the total collectibles of P5,948,950.90 for the whole year of 2017.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the municipal employees and officials responsible for the preparation and implementation/utilization of the budget of the municipality see to it that usage of the 20% Development Fund be in accordance with the set guidelines. For future reference and consistent with the aforecited joint circular, there is a new issuance of the DILG and DBM updating the guidelines on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund (DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017).
observations.managementAction: The utilization of the fund during the year includes projects, programs and activities that were not among the prescribed uses of the fund. However, it was explained by management that some of the PPAs considered by the audit team to be inconsistent with the set guidelines are already not included in the appropriation for their 20% Development Fund in 2018.
observations.reason:
Review of the agency’s report on the utilization of its 20% Development Fund revealed that several PPAs that were appropriated and utilized are inconsistent with the set guidelines.

Reiterated in Observation No. 12 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management closely monitor and enforce settlement of the audit disallowance. Henceforth, ensure that rules and regulations are strictly complied with before effecting payments to avoid disallowance in audit.
observations.managementAction: Concerned officials and employees are partially settling their respective shares in the disallowance through salary deduction. Management also agreed to closely monitor and enforce full settlement of the audit disallowance.
observations.reason:
Records of payments maintained by the Municipal Accountant shows that settlement for the said disallowance had already totaled to P245,000.00 as of the 1st quarter of 2018.

Reiterated in Observation No. 13 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor see to it that inventory taking be conducted and completed as soon as possible and a Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) be prepared and furnish the Auditor a copy for verification.

We have recommended that the municipal government request authority from the Commission on Audit to write-off the receivables in conformity with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 and the payables may be reverted in accordance with Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445 otherwise known as The Government Auditing Code of the Philippines.

We have recommended that the local officials assist and provide the Municipal Accountant with the required reports to facilitate the recording back in the books of accounts the 25% of the Public Infrastructures recorded in the registry as of December 31, 2015.  Likewise, the Local Chief Executive shall enjoin the LGU department heads compliance to COA Circular No. 2016-004 to facilitate the implementation of the accounting system and the New Chart of Accounts.

We have recommended that the Accountable Officers observe strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 on the liquidation of cash advances.  It should be noted that COA Circular No. 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012 provides sanctions that could be instituted to accountable officers to settle their cash advances.

We have recommended that the municipal government carefully review and faithfully adhere to the provisions of the Memorandum of Agreement to ensure the successful implementation of the proposed project and achieve the desired social outcome thereby, contributing to the government’s national goal of reducing poverty through the provision of additional employment to the constituents.
We have recommended that the municipal officials and employees strictly observe indication of time in the Itineraries of Travel to facilitate the verification of the validity of per diems claimed.

We have recommended that the municipal government adhere strictly to the provisions of RA 9184 and its revised IRR to ensure that procurement is effected in an efficient, economical and effective manner that is most advantageous to the government.

We have recommended that the municipal government strictly enforce the provision of Section 62.1 of the revised IRR of RA 9184 in order to fully protect the interest of the government in case of manufacturing defects.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer’s Office further intensify the collection of the delinquent realty taxes in previous years in order to increase income to carry out plans and objectives of the municipality.

We have recommended that the municipal employees and officials responsible for the preparation and implementation/utilization of the budget of the municipality see to it that usage of the 20% Development Fund be in accordance with the set guidelines. For future reference and consistent with the aforecited joint circular, there is a new issuance of the DILG and DBM updating the guidelines on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund (DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017).

We have recommended that management closely monitor and enforce settlement of the audit disallowance. Henceforth, ensure that rules and regulations are strictly complied with before effecting payments to avoid disallowance in audit.

We have recommended the Municipal Government to request authority from the Commission on Audit to write off the receivables in conformity with COA Resolution No. 2003-002 and the payables may be reverted in accordance with Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445 otherwise known as The Government Auditing Code of the Philippines.
observations.managementAction: Management had not observed the audit recommendation. Nevertheless, they signified their willingness to comply with it in order to clean the books of accounts of the agency and eventually fairly presenting the affected accounts in the financial statements.
observations.reason:
The   preparation of the needed documents for the receivables to be written off and the payables be reverted is still in-process.

Reiterated in Observation No. 2, p. 30 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balances of the accounts affected. Share of the municipality on local tobacco excise taxes should be recorded as Share from Tobacco Excise Tax.
observations.managementAction: For calendar year 2016, the share of the municipality  on local tobacco excise tax amounting to P35,768,520.00 was properly recorded as  income under Share from Tobacco Excise Tax account. However, the necessary  adjusting entries were not yet prepared by the Municipal Accountant to correct the  balances of the accounts affected in 2015.
observations.reason: Review of the journal entry vouchers as well as inquiry from the Municipal Accountant disclosed that no adjusting entries were prepared in relation to the erroneous recording in   the year 2015 of the LGU’s share from tobacco excise tax.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to enforce strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 on the liquidation of cash advances. Accountable officers must be reminded of the sanctions that may be instituted upon them as provided in COA Circular No. 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012, to wit:

Filing of malversation charge;
Suspension of salaries of erring accountable officers; and
Institution of appropriate administrative proceedings.
observations.managementAction: Management had sent demand letters to accountable officers who are  no longer with the municipal government yet,  no single reply  was received by  the municipal government.
observations.reason:
Review of the financial statements of the municipality revealed that the account Advances for Officers and Employees had unliquidated balance of P1,018,200.55 as of December 31, 2016.

Reiterated in Observation No. 4, p. 32 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the remaining PDAF with the municipality be returned to the National Treasury.
observations.managementAction: The municipal government had returned some of its unexpended PDAF.
observations.reason: It was noted in the verification of the year-end financial statements that only the total amount of P390.10 remained in the books of the municipality.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that disbursements out of the Petty Cash Fund shall be made through the use of a duly accomplished Petty Cash Voucher with complete supporting documents. Likewise, the Petty Cash Fund Custodian should liquidate fully the Petty Cash Fund at year-end.
observations.managementAction: For the year 2016, the municipal government adopted the use of the Petty Cash Vouchers in its petty cash transactions.
observations.reason: Based on post-audit of transactions of the municipal government for the year 2016, it was noted that Petty Cash Vouchers were already used for disbursements out of the Petty Cash Fund.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to intensify the collection of realty taxes in order to increase income to carry out plans and objectives of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: The municipality had exerted effort to collect almost all of the RPT and SET receivables  set up at the beginning of the year and agreed to further intensify its collection on the delinquent realty taxes in previous years.
observations.reason:
Analysis of general ledgers disclosed that of the total real property and special education tax receivables of P2,045,802.86 that were established to  be the amount       due and collectible for the year 2016, the municipality had collected a total of P1,809,195.98. Majority of the total year-end balance of P3,901,148.35 pertain to delinquent taxes in the previous years.

Reiterated in Observation No. 9, p. 38 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the appropriation and utilization of the 20% development fund be in accordance with the guidelines set under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011.
observations.managementAction:
For calendar year 2016, the municipality’s utilization of its 20% Development Fund includes projects, programs and activities that were not among  the prescribed uses of the fund. However, it was again asserted by management that development includes not only physical/structural projects in nature but also of human development as it is contributory and instrumental to an envisioned development  of the community as

 a whole.
observations.reason:
Review of the agency’s report on the utilization of its 20% Development Fund revealed that several PPAs that were appropriated and utilized are inconsistent with the set guidelines.

Reiterated in Observation No. 10, p. 39 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the concerned officials and employees to settle disallowances within the reglementary period as provided in COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated Sept. 15, 2009.
observations.managementAction: Concerned  officials and employees are partially settling their respective shares in the disallowance through salary deduction. Management also agreed to closely monitor and enforce full settlement of the audit disallowance.
observations.reason:
Based on inquiry, it was learned that records of the partial payments made are being maintained and monitored by the Municipal Accountant until full settlement is done.  

Reiterated in Observation No. 11, p. 41 of this report.
